February 2008
ASHRAE Journal
55
Lake-Source
District Cooling Systems
L
ake-source cooling (LSC), sometimes called deep lake
water cooling (DLWC), uses cold lake water instead of
chillers to provide cooling for large, multiple-building
applications such as urban communities or college campuses.
They typically extract cold lake water (from depths of 250
ft [76 m] or more) and circulate the lake water through heat
exchangers to remove heat from a district (or campus) chilled-
water loop (Figure 1). Auxiliary chillers may be used to supply
additional cooling as needed.
Energy Savings Potential
LSC systems realize large energy savings primarily by elimi-
nating (or reducing) the need for chiller-based cooling.\* Higher
pumping loads and thermal losses in the piping runs between
the lake and buildings served by the LSC offset a portion of
the energy savings. For example, a LSC at Cornell University
uses about 5 miles (8 km) of transmission piping that have
peak thermal losses of 800 tons (2 820 kW), or about 4% of the
20,000 ton (70 400 kW) total system cooling capacity.1,2
Limited data from recently installed LSC systems give
insight into the energy-savings potential of LSC. An LSC
system operating at Cornell University in Ithaca, N.Y. takes
water from 250 feet (76 m) below the surface of Cayuga Lake,
at a temperature of about 39°F (4°C). On an annual average,
it consumes about 0.10 kW per ton of cooling delivered, an
(0.03 kW/kW) is representative of the performance of LSC,
cooling energy savings would be about 83% relative to best
available conventional chiller systems. Savings compared to
air-cooled chillers would be higher.
U.S. commercial buildings consumed about 2.3 quadrillion
Btu in 2005 for space cooling.4 Of that, chillers consume about
31%,5 or about 0.7 quadrillion Btu. Of course, not all com-
mercial buildings are sufficiently close to a deep-water lake or
an ocean to use LSC. The authors estimate that roughly 10%
of the U.S. population lives in cities of more than 100,000 that
are on or near the ocean, a large ocean bay, or a Great Lake.
Assuming that LSC could address about 10% of the U.S.
commercial-building chiller load, the annual energy savings
would be about 0.06 quadrillion Btu, or about 8% of U.S. chiller
energy consumption.
Market Factors
A seawater-source cooling system proposed for Honolulu
is anticipated to deliver 25,000 tons (88 000 kW) cooling
capacity6 at a project cost of $145 million7, or $5,800/ton
($1,650/kW). This includes the cost of piping throughout the
service area (i.e., there is no preexisting distribution system).
The fresh-water LSC installed at Cornell University cost $58
million8 ($2,900/ton [$825/kW]). In this case, a chilled-water
distribution system already existed.
By Robert Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE;
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
eight-fold reduction (i.e., 87.5%) in
energy use in 2001 compared to the
previous cooling system’s 0.75 kW/
ton (0.21 kW/kW).1 Similarly, a LSC
system operating in Toronto using wa-
ter from Lake Ontario (at about 272 ft.
[83 m] below the surface) is reported
to use one-tenth the electricity used by
conventional chillers.3
In contrast, high-efficiency, water-
cooled chillers currently available can
achieve 0.5 kW/ton (0.14 kW/kW) or
lower. Adding in 0.10 kW/ton (0.03 kW/
kW) for cooling tower fans and pumps
results in about 0.6 kW/ton (0.17 kW/
kW) total. Assuming that 0.10 kW/ton

The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2008. ©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-

Fe b r u a r y 2 0 0 8
Based on 0.5 kW/ton (0.14 kW/kW) electricity savings and
a $500/ton ($142/kW) avoided cost for high-efficiency chillers,
and optimistically assuming 3,000 full-load-equivalent hours
per year and a commercial electricity price valued at $0.20/
kWh ($0.05/MJ) for space cooling (taking into account electric
rates during peak periods), the simple payback period would
range from about eight to 18 years. Of course, incentives could
lower the payback period significantly. Installation cost will
vary significantly depending on location, water quality/depth,
environmental considerations, and whether an existing district
cooling system is in place. Disruption of the community during
construction can also be a barrier.
Consideration of large-scale complex cooling systems such
as LSC requires coordination among a large group of stake-
holders, including local and state governments, environmental
and regulatory agencies, a district cooling system operator, and
local organizations that could use the cooling or heat delivered
by the LSC. Recognition of the benefits of reduced energy
consumption, reduced peak electric demand, and reduced CO2
and pollutants may justify providing economic incentives for
LSC installations.
One source estimates that there are 900 district energy
systems (heating and/or cooling systems) in North America,
including 5,000 miles (8000 km) of piping and serving 4.6
billion ft2 (427 million m2) of customer space.9 Only a small
fraction of these currently use LSC.
Perhaps the energy and environmental benefits of LSC will
encourage broader adoption of this energy-efficient approach
to cooling.

About the Authors
Photo © Mark Cramer
Rehabilitating
Health-Care
Facility
A
t Honoré-Mercier Hospital in Canada a serious Aspergillus
 contamination, resulting from spread of mildew within walls
caused by a deficient building envelope, was discovered in 2003.
The situation required decontamination and rehabilitation while
continuing to provide functional hospital services.
By Laurier Nichols, P.E., Fellow ASHRAE; & Charles Julien, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Cavities were created for the insulation
but ensuring moisture didn’t migrate was
sometimes neglected. For the Honoré-
Mercier Hospital, this neglect was the
source of a major Aspergillus outbreak.
The contamination was due to the spread
of mildew within the exterior walls.
The main component of the wall typical
to this hospital was precast concrete panels
attached to the main concrete structure. A
layer of 2 in. (50 mm) polystyrene board
was glued on the inside of the concrete
panel. A cavity was created between the
insulation surface and the inside wall fin-
The hospital is a 12-story, 300-bed
health-care facility that was built from
1969 to 1970. It is located in St. Hya-
cinthe (30 miles east of Montreal).
In the past, a high level of insulation
material was used to improve overall
thermal resistance of walls and roofs.
Massive masonry wall constructions pre-
viously were built using terracotta tiles,
bricks, granite blocks and a thin layer
of insulation made of cork or fiberglass.
This type of construction had a poor
thermal resistance (in the range of R-6
[Rsi-1.06]), but the inside plaster finish
was relatively tight to air leakage.
New insulation materials were devel-
oped in the late 1960s that required a
special wall construction to incorporate
the insulation material with the other
components of the wall composition.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2008. ©Copyright 2008
American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is
presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed
electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2008
ASHRAE Journal
29
ish. This cavity was used to locate the HVAC terminal boxes. The
inside finish was made of two layers of .05 in. (13 mm) gypsum
board. Steel panels were used to provide access to HVAC com-
ponents inside the walls. Aluminum windows were attached to
the concrete panel with wood framing (Figure 1).
In theory, the wall should have performed well. However, it
required proper installation and adequate maintenance of wall
components. Over the years, the joints between the concrete wall
panels and other wall components deteriorated and water leaked
inside the wall system. Water could infiltrate around the window
framing. The aluminum window also let water penetrate because
of aging gaskets around the operable sections. Hot water heating
components also leaked on the wall components.
On cold days, frost developed between the concrete panels and
the insulation boards. And, neither surface could tighten together
exactly. Over the years, because of the sustained presence of water
in the wall, the wood parts rotted and Aspergillus developed in the
cavities of the exterior wall (Photos 1 and 2).
Other problems were identified. The presence of
exposed combustible materials (wood, polystyrene,
etc.) in the wall composition could cause a major
fire spread. Also, fire containment from floor to
floor was not adequate because of the wood com-
ponents used for drape support on the top edge near
the windows (Figure 1).
The investigation found several attachments of con-
crete panels to the building infrastructure had to be modified.
The anchors had to account for expansion and contraction of
panels due to the extreme variation of the weather conditions.
Another issue was the high energy use of the terminal re-
heat systems used for air conditioning throughout the hospital
services. The rehabilitation provided a good opportunity to
modify all the HVAC systems to improve filtration efficiency
and energy performance.
Solving the Problem
Identifying solutions to correct major deficiencies was a
challenge when health-care services had to continue for the
surrounding population. The question was, should the building
close? It meant another facility needed to provide health-care
services during the renovations. It was certainly not the best
solution because the core medical group had to be located
near the hospital complex. The plan was that rehabilitation
would be done while all health-care services were offered on
a continuous basis.
The rehabilitation was done in three major phases (Figure 2):
Phase 1 Construction of a new 90,000 ft2 (9000 m²) build-
ing (Hervé-Gagnon Pavilion) to relocate the specialized
services: intensive care, operating rooms, radiology,
sterilization, laboratories, etc. Some services (such
as the laboratories) were permanently relocated in
the new pavilion (Hervé-Gagnon).
Temporary buildings for the 60,000 ft2 (5575
m2) emergency room, ambulatory services, adminis-
tration, oncology, external clinics, cafeteria, etc., were
constructed. Food preparation was outsourced.
The adjacent St. Charles Pavilion was rehabilitated to
accomodate the patient care units (300 beds). This is a former
135,000 ft2 (12 500 m2) hospital built in 1929.
Phase 2 All services were relocated in the adjacent new
buildings and the St. Charles Pavilion. All services were closed
at Honoré-Mercier Pavilion and the building was emptied.
Honoré-Mercier Pavilion, the main building, was decontami-
nated and rehabilitated.
Phase 3 All services were reintroduced into the rehabilitated
Honoré-Mercier Pavilion: patient care units, obstetrics, emer-
gency, operating rooms, radiology, etc.
The new building (Hervé-Gagnon Pavilion) was refitted for
the permanent services: laboratories, cafeteria, ambulatory,
imaging, ambulatory services, etc.

Photo 2: Rotten wood.

30
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
The old St. Charles Pavilion was refitted for psychiatric care
and administrative services. This created a major change in the
appearance of the building (see photos on title page).
Honoré-Mercier Pavilion
Hervé-Gagnon Pavilion
St. Charles Pavilion
Figure 2: Rehabilitation at the Honoré-Mercier Hospital was completed in three major phases: construction of a new building, the Hervé-
Gagnon Pavilion, rehabilitation of the St. Charles Pavilion, and rehabilitation of the Honoré-Mercier Pavilion.
Decontamination
Most areas in the Honoré-Mercier Hospital were high risk
for patients with a low immune system. Confinement and
protection measures to fight contamination were a major
challenge because of the necessity to maintain negative and
positive pressures, at all times, between the working areas and
the decontaminated ones.
The contamination was spread at an 8 ft (2.5 m) depth all
around the building envelope (exterior walls). All the perim-
eter spaces were provided with a containment enclosure with
continuous negative pressure conditions. The outside enclosure
was the new permanent curtain wall attached to the concrete
panels and the inside enclosure was made of temporary
double polyethylene film. The negative pressure was created
by exhaust fan equipped with HEPA filtration. Using pres-
sure sensors and constant monitoring of pressure differential,
a specialized control system was used to ensure a complete
stronghold of contaminated areas.
All partition walls on the perimeter were demolished and
the inside components of the exterior walls were removed.
All surfaces were sprayed and cleaned with compressed CO2.
A specially trained sniffer dog was used to detect any other
additional spaces where Aspergillus contamination could be
present. Several surfaces close to bathrooms and shower rooms
were contaminated. All the construction workers wore special
masks and clothing during the decontamination processes.
All the modified HVAC and new HVAC systems were
equipped with HEPA filter downstream of any component
in the distribution system. Acoustical insulation was banned
inside any length of duct.
Improvement of Energy Efficiency
Because they require a high rate of air changes per hour and
have a large quantity of medical equipment, health centers are
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
32
ASHRAE Journal
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
among the most energy-demanding buildings. Energy con-
sumption studies in existing health-care facilities have shown
that some buildings have energy use as high as 87.5 kWh/ft2
(3.5 GJ/m2).
Since this project called for a complete rebuilding of the
HVAC systems of the health center, it was a good opportu-
nity to completely revise the systems. The energy-efficiency
measures included enthalpy wheels and runaround coils,
which recover heat from exhaust air to be used for outside
ventilation air preheating. Other measures include low tem-
perature heating loop, heat recovery chillers, high efficiency
boilers, efficient lighting systems, etc.
One major terminal reheat system provided air conditioning
for all patient room care units (10 stories). It was a 65,000
cfm (30 700 L/s) system supplying
air in the interior zones, as well as
the north exterior zone (12 rooms per
floor dedicated to patients’ care) and
the south exterior zone (12 rooms per
floor dedicated to patients’ care). The
air-conditioning load of the interior
zone required that the primary air sup-
plied be maintained at 55°F (12.8°C)
at all times. It is easy to understand the
requirement for reheat in the winter
season on the north perimeter zone.
During most winter seasons, the north
zone and even the south perimeter zone
required reheat.
The supply fan discharge of this
large system was split into three
separate cooling sections: one for the
interior zone, one for the north zone
and one for the south zone. There is
a dedicated outside air system to sup-
ply air to this large system. First, the
outside air is heated by heat recovery
on the exhaust air, and second, the
outside air is heated by a condensing
heat exchanger on the flue gas of the
boilers.
The result is a reduction in energy
use of up to $1 million annually, and
a savings of 3,500 tons a year in
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The
entire complex uses 42 kWh/ft2 (1.68
Gj/m²) per year.
Project Cost
The total project cost of the reha-
bilitation was $124 million of which
$60 million was dedicated to the me-
chanical and electrical services. This
cost includes some of the expenses
associated with the relocation of ser-
vices and the moving of specialized
equipments.
The cost to renovate the existing
Honoré-Mercier Hospital was a major
expenditure and shows that existing
infrastructures must be built to last
longer and be properly maintained.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
22
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2008
First Place Winners
Albert R. Barfield, Member ASHRAE
Project: Springhill Suites, Pensacola, Fla.
Category: Commercial Buildings (New)
This hotel on a barrier island on the Florida Gulf Coast uses a
hybrid geothermal system, which features a 150 ton (530 kW)
closed-loop evaporative fluid cooler. The loop field is set up in
parallel with the 150 ton (530 kW) fluid cooler, which offers
considerable heat rejection control and redundancy. Primary
domestic water heaters and water-to-water geothermal
heat pumps are used. All pool and spa heating is provided
by geothermal heat pumps. In addition, more than 300 tons
(1055 kW) of room unitary, ducted geothermal heat pumps
are used in guest suites and to serve all other conditioned
areas of the hotel.
Overall annual energy intensity for this hybrid geothermal hotel
is 79 kBtu/ft2 (897 MJ/m­2). By comparison, another hotel in the
same geographic location, but with air-source equipment, operates
at an annual energy intensity of 139 kBtu/ft2 (1.6 GJ/m­2).
Craig A. Watts, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Project: Fossil Ridge High School, Fort Collins, Colo.
Category: Institutional Buildings (New)
Functioning as a small community in itself, Fossil Ridge High School
has several types of mechanical systems ranging in size from small
DX split systems for computer room cooling to large air handlers
with energy recovery devices for spaces such as the auditorium.
When operated as a package, these components provide the school
with an innovative and energy-efficient mechanical system. The
demand-controlled classroom ventilation via occupancy sensors
and window sensors allows for reduced energy consumption by
eliminating the treatment of unneeded outside air, while providing
occupants with the ability to bring in naturally ventilated outside
air when they feel it is appropriate.
The value of the energy savings from the mechanical, plumbing,
irrigation and building lighting systems projected over the
building’s lifespan is estimated at more than $6 million at current
utility rates.
Rick L. Grove, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Project: Firstenburg Community Center, Vancouver,
Wash.
Category: Public Assembly (New)
The community center is a two-story, 72,500 ft2 (6735 m2), multi-
use facility that includes a fitness area, aquatics space, meeting
rooms, lounges, administration areas and a gymnasium.
The majority of the spaces use either natural ventilation, assisted
ventilation or a hybrid system. Two 97% thermally efficient natural
gas condensing boilers, each sized to 50% of the full heating
requirement, provide heating water for domestic, pool, building
skin and ventilation heating loads. Another sustainable feature
is that gray water collected from the pools’ backwash filters is
captured for use in flushing toilets, decreasing the amount of water
T
   he ASHRAE Technology Awards recognize
outstanding achievements by members who
have successfully applied innovative build-
ing design in the areas of occupant comfort, indoor air
quality (IAQ) and energy conservation.
Their designs incorporate ASHRAE standards for effective
energy management and IAQ. Performance is proven through one year’s actual, verifiable
operating data. The following are descriptions of projects from the ASHRAE Technology
Award winners and honorable mentions. Articles about these projects will be published
in this and future issues of ASHRAE Journal.
Outstanding Achievements

Springhill Suites
This hotel uses a hybrid geothermal system, which
features a 150 ton (530 kW) closed-loop evaporative
fluid cooler. All pool and spa heating is provided by
geothermal heat pumps.
Fossil Ridge High School
Fossil Ridge High School has several types of mechanical systems
ranging in size from small DX split systems for computer room cooling
to large air handlers with energy recovery devices for spaces such as
the auditorium.
Firstenburg Community Center
Overhangs, sunscreens, and adjacent mature fir trees
minimize direct solar heat gain during the cooling
season.
Honoré-Mercier Hospital
The HVAC systems in this hospital were completely revised
during a major renovation. Features include enthalpy wheels and
runaround coils.

introduced into the municipal sanitary system by some 500,000

Thermal analysis models were developed to validate the
natural ventilation design. An energy model of the building based
on construction documents and documented actual operating
assumptions showed 26% energy savings as compared to a
Washington state baseline model.
Apichit Lumlertpongpana, Member ASHRAE, and Wichai
Rungruangprug, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Charoen Pokphan Foods Public Company Ltd.,
Chokchai, Thailand
Category: Industrial Facilities or Processes (New)
The design focused on a refrigeration system and low-temperature
air conditioning for a poultry slaughter and processing plant, with
a slaughter capacity of 360,000 birds per day.
By installing smaller compressors, smaller suction pipes and
insulation material, the system saves some $200,000 of the first
cost of equipment and material. The estimated annual energy
savings is nearly $300,000 a year, with an estimated total reduction
of carbon dioxide emissions equal to 1,900 metric tons (2,094 short
tons) per year. Water use was reduced by 91.3%.
Laurier Nichols, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Rejean Blais,
Member ASHRAE; Simon Pelletier, P.E., Member ASHRAE;
Oanh Nguyen; Jean Molina, Member ASHRAE; and Marco
Freitas, Member ASHRAE
Project: Rehabilitation of Honoré-Mercier Hospital,
Saint-Hyacinthe, QC, Canada

At Honoré-Mercier Hospital in Canada, a serious Aspergillus
contamination, resulting from spread of mildew within walls, which
was caused by a deficient building envelope, was discovered in
2003. The situation required decontamination and rehabilitation
Outstanding Achievements
Environmental Discovery Center
Heat pumps use well water on the way to the pond or water
sprayground as a heat sink. Because the well water is always
50°F – 55°F (10°C – 13°C), cooling is efficient.

while continuing to provide functional hospital services. The project
required temporary relocation of 300 beds to an adjacent hospital
that had not been used since the 1990s. It also required use of 60,000
ft2 (5574 m2) of temporary facilities for other hospital services.
The design team offered integrated solutions where energy
efficiency and the principles of sustainable development were
determining criteria for selection of designs based on a life-cycle cost
approach. This led to a high-performance building whose greenhouse
gas emissions have been reduced by 3,576 tons (1622 kg) per year.

David Kistler, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Project: Environmental Discovery Center, Springfield
Township, Mich.
Category: Public Assembly (New)
The 19,000 ft­2 (1765 m2) Environmental Discovery Center allows
visitors to view the Kettle Pond from above water, at the water,
and below the water. Heating and cooling is provided by 30 ton
(106 kW) and 10 ton (35 kW) water-to-water heat pumps. The
heat pumps provide warm and chilled water to fan coil units
located near each space. The heat pumps also use the well
water on the way to the kettle pond or water sprayground
as a heat sink. The building consumed only 228,320 kWh per
year (does not include the separately metered well pumps).

Pierre Roussel, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Complexe les Ailes, Montreal, QC, Canada
Category: Commercial Buildings (Existing)
This retail and office complex in downtown Montreal was
renovated using heat recovery throughout the system, eliminating
the need for installing one hot water boiler and two steam boilers
for humidification. The fresh air system has a preheating coil and
heat coil. The heat required for the preheating coil in the fresh
air system originates from the chilled water loop via a plate heat
exchanger and glycol solution. This arrangement supplies chilled
water without the use of chillers.
Stephane Sirard, Member ASHRAE
Project: Centre de Formation Professionnelle l’Horizon,
Mont-Tremblant, QC, Canada
Category: Institutional Buildings (New)
The Horizon Professional Center is a specialized school that
includes three 2,000 ft2 (186 m2) greenhouses. Solar and ground
energy were chosen as the principal heat sources using geothermal
systems and a solar wall. The designers used an indirect vertical
exchange so the system could be used for the main school building
and the greenhouses. A thermal storage system was used for the
greenhouses by storing the available energy during sunny periods in
the ground directly under the greenhouse for later use.
Christopher Rauch, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Aramark Chicago Industrial Laundry, Chicago
Category: Industrial Facilities or Processes (New)
In 2003–2004, Aramark replaced its aging, multistory industrial
laundry facility with a new 125,000 ft2 (11 613 m2) single-story
facility. Design elements include a wastewater reuse heat recovery
system. From the 800 lb (363 kg) capacity washers, 125°F (52°C)
wastewater discharge is piped through a manifolded reuse system
to a heat exchanger system, which captures 24% of the steam
boiler plant’s input and transfers the recovered heat back to the
makeup and tempered domestic hot water streams.
Steven Jones, Associate Member ASHRAE
Project: Kern Schools Federal Credit Union Administration
Building, Bakersfield, Calif.
Category: Commercial Buildings (New)
This pair of three-story buildings, each 75,000 ft­2 (6968 m2),
exceeded California’s Title 24 Energy Standards by 24%. Each
building is conditioned by three rooftop packaged VAV air handlers
using evaporative cooled condensers. Building heating is provided
by variable flow, high-efficiency condensing pulse boilers, circulating
hot water to each of the VAV terminal units. The first year energy
use was measured to be 50% less than a building of similar size and
occupancy owned by the same property manager.

About the Authors
Air Conditioning
Remote Telecom
Enclosures
By Abdel-Kader Darwiche, CEng, Member ASHRAE;
And Samivullah Shaik
P
ipelines and supply networks for datacom services often
run through remote areas such as deserts, which are far
Load Calculation
The air-conditioning system design
must be tailored to the type of environ-
mental conditions the telecom shelter en-
counters, as well as the heat generation by
the equipment installed inside the shelter.
The goal of the HVAC systems designer
is to maintain the peak temperatures in
the shelter below a certain level, which
is normally prescribed by the electronic
equipment manufacturer. Humidity levels
are a concern, but because most shelters
are sealed and their temperatures are
much higher than the air’s dew point,
humidity generally is not a problem (after
the transient effect of opening and closing
the enclosure is eliminated). Other ambi-
ent conditions affecting the air condition
design include: solar radiation, wind
speeds, objects surrounding the shelter
(shading, ground reflections, buildings,
and trees) and shelter design (surface
area, shape, paint).
The first step is to perform a load cal-
culation to size the equipment. This can
be calculated using any of the methods
prescribed in the 2005 ASHRAE Hand-
book—Fundamentals. Alternatively, the
load calculation can be performed using
any commercial load calculation software
that adopts any ASHRAE-approved meth-
ods. Exercise care when inside design
temperatures exceed the outdoor design
temperatures due to heat buildup. Most
software cannot accept such input.
removed from conventional power lines. Therefore, communi-
cation along the pipeline or between networks necessitates
telecom repeaters or relay stations in remote locations. The
daily air temperatures can reach 122°F (50°C) in high ambi-
ent desert environments, which combined with internal heat
generation from the electronic equipment, make the inside
of the telecom shelter prone to temperatures as high as
176°F (80°C). In these conditions, electronic equipment will
not function reliably. This presents a significant challenge to
the HVAC system designer.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2008. ©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2008
ASHRAE Journal
43
Passive Cooling Systems
Passive methods include primarily natural (free) convection
and phase changing materials (PCMs).
Natural (Free) Convection
This system is widely used, although it is expected to be re-
placed with more advanced systems.
This technique does not require a power source because it
functions on natural convection and energy storage capacity. This
system (Figure 2) consists of housings with insulation, sunshade,
external and internal heat exchangers, heat storage tank and con-
veyance of the medium (mainly water), which must be designed
for the specific application. The heat dissipated by the electronic
equipment is absorbed by the internal heat exchanger and trans-
ferred to the water inside the tank by thermosiphon. At night, heat
is dissipated by thermosiphon to the outside as illustrated in Figures
3a and 3b. The temperature inside the shelter can be maintained
from 15°F to 20°F (8.3°C to 11.1°C) below ambient.
The free convection passive cooling system basically levels
off the temperature inside the shelter by temperature dampening.
Figure 4 shows the expected inside temperature of a passively
cooled shelter located in a typical high ambient desert environ-
ment compared to the outdoor air. The tank and heat exchangers’
temperatures are also shown on the same figure.4
The main advantage of this system is that no energy is re-
Figure 2: Principle of free convection passive cooling system.
Heat Storage
Tank
Equipment
Shelter
Ambient Air
Internal Heat
Exchangers
External Heat
Exchangers
Working Fluid
Figure 1: Vortex cooler.1
Compressed Air
Cold Stream
Hot Stream
Once the required cooling capacity of the equipment is ob-
tained, selecting the equipment or system type is the most impor-
tant task that faces the HVAC designer, especially when limited
or no electrical power is available. The following information
reviews the systems available for the designer.
Ventilation by Exhaust Fans
This is probably the oldest technique to control the tempera-
ture inside remote telecom shelters by ventilating the air using
simple exhaust fans. These exhaust fans, which in most cases
operate on direct current (dc) powered by batteries, prevent heat
buildup by circulating the outside air inside the telecom shelter.
The airflow usually is designed to offset heat generation by the
equipment, keeping the temperature around 5°F to 10°F (3°C
to 6°C) above ambient. This high temperature, coupled with
the particles (mainly sand) in the outdoor air, result in reduced
equipment life, making this an unpopular design option. This
option is sometimes used in battery enclosures that are adjacent
to the main telecom enclosures. In this case, the fans also prevent
hydrogen generated from the batteries from accumulating inside
the battery enclosures.
Vortex Coolers
The vortex cooler, shown in Figure 1,1 is a tube, also known as
the Ranque-Hilsch vortex tube that separates gas (usually com-
pressed air) into hot and cold streams. Pressurized gas is injected
into a swirl chamber and accelerates to a high rate of rotation
(more than 1 million rpm). The gas is split into two streams, one
giving kinetic energy to the other, and resulting in separate flows
of hot and cold gases. The cold gas is injected into the shelter
while the hot gas is expelled outside.
Applications include spot cooling a cabinet that generates
high heat in a shelter. Vortex coolers are rarely used to cool large
shelters because they are usually limited in capacity to about
5,000 Btu/h (1.46 kW).
The vortex cooler is also characterized by a lack of moving
parts and low efficiency, although they are limited in size and
depend on the availability of compressed air.
Peltier Cooler
Peltier coolers use the Peltier effect, which is “the evolution
or absorption of heat produced by an electric current passing
across junctions of two suitable dissimilar metals, alloys or
semiconductors.”2
“The coolers are small solid-state devices that function as a
heat pump. …When a dc current is applied heat is moved from
one side of the device to the other—where it must be removed
with a heatsink.”3
Thermoelectric junctions are generally around 5% to 10% as
efficient as the ideal refrigerator Carnot cycle), compared with
40% to 60% achieved by conventional compression cycle systems
(reverse Rankine systems like a compressor).
As with the vortex cooler, Peltier coolers are better candi-
dates for spot cooling and are seldom used to cool an entire
shelter.
44
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
44
ASHRAE Journal
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
conditioning system. The PCMs will act as a thermal storage
during the normal operation of this system. In case the system
fails, PCMs will absorb the heat generated inside the shelter,
preventing heat buildup for as long as necessary until the main
air-conditioning system can be reengaged.
PCMs can act as the main air-conditioning system (Figure 5)
for a shelter if a sufficient temperature difference exists between
day and night. In this case, PCMs store energy during the day
from the shelter and release to the atmosphere at night.
The most commonly used PCMs are salt hydrides, fatty acids,
esters and paraffins.
dc-Powered Air-Conditioning System
Air conditioning telecom shelters to an inside temperature
near human comfort levels (75°F [24°C]) is challenging for
quired to run it. In addition, it is highly reliable because there
are no moving mechanical parts. The main disadvantage is the
large space required to install this system and the higher initial
cost. This system’s performance is highly dependent on high
temperature differences between day and night (typical for
desert environment). It is not well suited for environments in
which temperatures do not vary much over the day.
In such places, some manufacturers have added small dc-
operated pumps, to help the free convection. In instances
where the difference in temperature between the shelter and
the outside temperature is not sufficient to invoke natural
convection, these pumps work to circulate the fluid between
the heat exchangers. Although this negates one of the main
advantages of this system (it has no moving parts), it allows
this system to be used in more mild environments.
Phase Change Materials (PCMs)
PCMs usually are stored in various sizes and shapes of
containers although generally they perform best in small ones.
Typical container configurations are wall panels, steel pipes,
aluminum cylinders, or plastic balls. It’s important for the
material to be conductive, impermeable and flexible to accom-
modate volume changes.
PCMs are extensively used as a backup to the main air-

are beyond the scope of this article. The reader can refer to
manufacturer data6 for further details. Figure 6 shows one
dc-powered air-conditioning unit installed inside a remote
telecom shelter.
These new units are available to operate on either 24V or 48V.
Combined with a battery system charged through solar panels,
these units can be used to air condition remote telecom shelters
to a human comfort zone.
One disadvantage of this system is that the footprint re-
quired to install the solar panels is larger than conventional
systems. However, in remote locations, land is often readily
available. Another disadvantage is that this system contains
moving parts, which require some maintenance. However,
these disadvantages outweigh the cooling level this system is
offers. Therefore, HVAC designers are increasingly selecting
the system.
Conclusion
Designing the cooling system for a remote telecom shelter
presents a great challenge to the designer. In addition, with
the increasing heat densities, the performance requirements
and capacities of the air-conditioning system is become more
demanding. Many conventional ways have existed to overcome
some of these challenges. Moreover, recent technological in-
novations have provided the HVAC designer with a broader
range of equipment availability with larger capacities. Table 1
summarizes the advantages and disadvantages of each avail-
able system.

1. Vortex tube public domain image from http://en.wikipedia.

As an example, a dc-powered air conditioning system
is planned for a remote shelter in a high ambient desert
environment. The shelter total floor area is about 284 ft2
(26.4 m2). The following are the steps followed for sizing
the system. The electrical portion of the calculations should
be performed in conjunction with the project’s electrical
engineer.
The outdoor/indoor environmental conditions were
obtained from published data.
Outdoor Conditions: 115°F (46°C) DBT
Indoor Conditions: 72.5°F (22.5°C) DBT
The shelter’s floor plan and elevations were obtained
from the architect.
The heat generation from the installed equipment was
obtained from the equipment manufacturer.
Commercial HVAC load calculation software was used to
estimate the required cooling capacity for each hour. The
software output indicated that the shelter has a maximum
cooling of load of 11,800 Btu/h (3.46 kW) at 3 p.m. for a
typical summer day.
Manufacturer’s catalogue indicates that at the design
outdoor conditions, the dc-powered air-conditioning unit
will deliver 6,500 Btu/h (1.9 kW). Therefore, two units are
required.
The total energy required during a summer day is required
to size the batteries and solar array. The power consump-
tion of the selected unit highly depends on the outside
temperature, which varies widely during the day. The power
consumed by the units at various outdoor air temperatures
was obtained from the manufacturer.
A spreadsheet was used to estimate the energy consumed
in that typical summer day. Alternatively, energy simulation
software can be used. The result yielded a power consump-
tion of 19.407 kWh per day.
Using a value of seven hours of sun-hours per day (data
obtained from the end user for this site) and 100 W per
each solar panel,
The total solar array capacity required:
19,407 ÷ 7 = 2,772.5 W;
Assuming 10% cable losses, net solar array capacity
required is 3,049 W; and
The number of panels required:
3049 ÷ 100 = 31 panels.
Of course, the previous figures are location specific and
change from site to site depending on the available sun-hours
per day and the probability of cloudy periods.
Using the daily energy consumption obtained earlier and
using a 48V dc voltage:
The daily current consumption:
19,407.8 ÷ 48 = 404 ampere-hour per day;
Assuming a 10% safety factor, the daily current consump-
tion is 444 ampere-hour per day;
Assuming five days autonomy, the total required current
is 444 x 5 = 2,220 ampere-hour; and
Using 12V-230 ampere-hour batteries, four numbers are
required in series to obtain 48V and 2220 ÷ 230 = 9.65
(10) are required to provide the current, so a total of 40
batteries are required.
Sizing, Selecting dc-Powered Air-Conditioning Systems

March 2008
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
H
undreds of bullet holes were found when the roof of
the Superdome was replaced after Hurricane Katrina.
Over the years, the large, white Superdome roof that
dominated the New Orleans skyline apparently proved to be an
irresistible target for folks with weapons.
Now, I am a simple kind of guy. I bet that a roof with hun-
dreds of bullet holes probably leaked. I wonder what those holes
did to the Superdome’s roof ?
I remind everyone that the roof of the Superdome experi-
enced one of the more spectacular failures in enclosure his-
tory. It was ripped off during Katrina in less than catastroph-
ic wind conditions (Photo 1). Let’s toy with this memorable
failure a little. Hypothetically, because there is litigation
ongoing.\*
It is quite something when a roof blows off. To have one
blow off during a national disaster with the world watching,
puts it into a class by itself. However, the roof blowing off the
Superdome is not an isolated incident. We seem to be experi-
encing more uplift problems with roofs lately. Why?
We seem to have forgotten how to build roofs. Back in
the day, compact roofs required multiple layers. One of the
most important was the air barrier (Figure 1). Air barriers
were originally an artifact of cold climate construction and
often misnamed as vapor barriers. In Canada, where there are
only two seasons, this winter and last winter, architects and
engineers learned about the importance of air barriers in con-
trolling roof assembly moisture-induced deterioration from
interior sources. However, early on, one of the most impor-
tant lessons of using air barriers was the huge improvement in
uplift performance. The air barrier helped to transfer the wind
load to the structural deck. And, this proved to be important in
all climates besides those dominated by hockey players.
When mechanically attached membranes became com-
mon—a bad idea in my humble opinion, the lack of an air bar-
rier lead to fluttering of membranes (Figure 2), some interesting
failures (Photo 2) and some interesting solutions (Photo 3).
Fluttering, besides stressing the membrane, leads to the pump-
ing transfer of airborne moisture from the interior into the roof
assembly. This has typically been a cold climate phenomenon but
the problems are migrating south as membranes become white,
How Not to Build Roofs
Photo 1: This never should have happened. The Superdome’s roof membrane is missing after Hurricane Katrina in 2005.
\*There is always litigation. I am not involved, and I don’t know any of the parties. I have not talked to anyone involved. However, I am bemused and curious, which
allows me to explore avenues closed to well-mannered folk in polite society. I can speculate, a dirty word for physicists, but an honorable one for engineers. All of
the information presented comes from the Internet or my own observations and experience. No one leaked information. The only thing that leaked was the roof.

Photo 2: Fluttering membrane. No air barrier at deck leads to an
idea for a Disney World ride.
Photo 3: Fluttering membrane fix. “I’ll fix that darn moving mem-
brane. Get me some pavers and blocks.”
Membrane Flutters
Air Leakage
Figure 2: Fluttering membrane. Mechanically attached membrane
flutters due to air leakage from interior when air barrier is omitted.

Figure 1: Multiple layer compact roof. Mechanically attached sheath-
ing supports fully adhered air barrier. Fully adhered insulation boards
have the best performance when split into two layers with joints stag-
gered to further limit three dimensional airflow paths. Fully adhered
coverboard protects insulation and facilitates lateral migration of water
vapor and other off-gassing products to control blisters. It is becoming
increasingly common to mechanically attach the insulation layers and
membrane while also omitting the coverboard, which is an unfortunate
development. Some insurance policies require mechanically attached
layers because they don’t trust the workmanship necessary for full ad-
hesion of layers. Old-timers roll their eyes at this. Crotchety old-timers
do both. They fully adhere the layers to get the performance and add
the mechanical attachment to shut the insurance folks up.
rather than black. Dark membranes become hot, and the heat
drives the moisture back down into the building. In the south,
mechanically attached dark membranes roofs typically dodged
the moisture bullet because the moisture that was pumped up was
driven back down by the huge temperature gradient. However,
that all began to change with energy conservation and light-col-
ored membranes. Many roofs no longer get hot enough to drive
flutter-driven moisture back down into the building. Failures that
were limited to cold climates now happen in Georgia.
Air barriers make fluttering go away and make the world
safe for mechanically attached light colored membranes. The
fluttering is controlled since any attempt by the membrane to
lift off the insulation layer is resisted by suction. Air from the
interior is not able to enter the roof assembly due to the pres-
ence of the air barrier. The lack of an air barrier and a leaky
deck will allow replacement air to enter the roof assembly
from the interior and the suction resistance is lost.
Uplift resistance is increased with the presence of an air
barrier by the transfer of a significant amount of the load from
the membrane to the air barrier and deck. A little algebra il-
lustrates the principle. The total wind pressure is taken by all
of the roof elements acting together. The total wind pressure
resisted by the roof assembly is equal to the sum of the indi-
vidual pressures taken by each element. In general, the tighter
the element, the greater the pressure taken by the element. A
simple series pressure relationship is a pretty reasonable ap-
proximation of what actually occurs.

+

where
Pt = total wind pressure
Pm = membrane pressure
Pi = insulation pressure
Pd = deck pressure
Here is a trick question: what is the tightest element of a
roof ? That would be the element without any holes, which is
the membrane since it can’t have holes. Otherwise, it leaks.
So, if I have a tight membrane, rigid insulation installed in
boards/sheets with gaps over a fluted steel deck with seams
and holes, the majority of the wind load is taken by the mem-
brane. That was the case in the Superdome roof (Figure 3).
How do we know that? Well, the membrane is gone, and the
insulation was left behind (Photo 4).
With an air barrier, a significant portion of the wind pres-
sure is transferred to the air barrier and its attachment to the
deck. The tighter the air barrier, the greater the load transfer.
The greater the load transfer to the air barrier, the less stress
62
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
March 2008
on the membrane. Figure 1 rocks, Fig-
ure 3 sucks (literally, wind suction).
The Superdome roof did not have an
was reported that the smoke dampers at
the top of the roof were sucked off dur-
ing Katrina and that allowed the wind to
enter, exacerbating building pressuriza-
tion and stressing the roof assembly. The
membrane took the majority of the load,
unzipped and traveled to a neighboring
parish.
Could it be that the lack of an air bar-
rier caused the Superdome roof to fail
in wind conditions that were pretty mi-
nor as far as hurricanes go? I’m asking
hypothetically. And, how was the Su-
perdome replacement roof that was in-
stalled in 2002 allowed to be constructed
without an air barrier when the original
roof that was installed in the 1980s had
one? Where were the adults?
Let’s return to the bullet holes. It may
be surprising to folks that people shoot at

that allowed lateral airflow and lateral water migration.

air barrier. At least the replacement roof
installed in 2002 didn’t have one, which
is the roof that failed during Katrina. It

64
ASHRAE Journal

March 2008
domed stadium roofs in many areas, regardless of team affili-
ations. So, specifically, what about the bullet holes at the Su-
perdome? Well, the steel decking was so corroded it had to be
replaced after the Katrina blow-off. The steel decking was fine
in 2002 during the re-roof. It wasn’t fine in 2005. The water
came from somewhere. Could it be that hundreds of bullet holes
could be a factor? I’m asking hypothetically.
The good news is that the post-Katrina repair is bulletproof †
and has an air barrier. The new roof is similar to the original roof.
It is a spray polyurethane insulation system applied directly to the
roof deck (Figure 4 and Photo 5). A spray applied fully adhered
membrane is installed over the top of the insulation (Photo 6).
The spray polyurethane insulation also acts as the air barrier.
So, how are bullets handled in the new roof? The bullet goes
right through the roof system providing a straight-through-the-
assembly water path. The spray foam does not allow the lateral
migration of water that occurred in the 2002 roof (Figure 5).
So, the roof isn’t really bulletproof, but bullets don’t cause
Photo 6: Better than before. Welcome back Saints.
† I would have given anything to have been a fly on the wall when the engineer
or architect of record was told that not only did the post-Katrina Superdome
replacement roof need to resist uplift but that it also had to deal with bullets. I do
not know who the engineers or architects were on the repair and replacement
project, but I salute them. They succeeded on both fronts. Well done. Very well
done indeed.
anything besides a minor annoyance. The bullet holes are easy
to fix—the hole and the leak are coincident—and corrosion is
limited and controlled.

Figure 5: Bulletproof. Bullet holes in the left-hand roof section allow for lateral migration of rainwater due to the gaps in the roof system
layers and the channels provided by the fluted steel deck. In the right-hand roof section bullet holes result in a straight-through-the as-
sembly flow path for water, thereby limiting damage to the hole only.
Would there have been any difference in the Superdome roof
blow-off if there were no bullet holes and no corrosion of the
underlying deck? Probably not. The only thing that might have
changed on the repair side would have been the deck replace-
ment. However, the argument has been made that the deck need-
ed upgrading regardless to handle even greater wind loads.
Can we learn any lessons from all of this? Lots of roofs
today are being constructed without air barriers or have been
constructed without air barriers. I predict many uplifting mo-
ments in our future unless air barriers become common (to
say nothing of the problems of fluttering membranes and light
colored roofs).

66
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
Ongoing Commissioning
A
lthough most building HVAC systems maintain basic
levels of functionality, many suffer from a depar-
ture from intended performance due to a range of
“faults” that compromise their effectiveness, degrade climate
control, and increase building energy consumption. Build-
ing commissioning can effectively address these issues. It
is the process of measuring, observing and evaluating the
performance of building systems to identify substandard
function of equipment, sensors and systems. Commissioning
also can include tuning equipment and systems, including
their controls, to enhance performance above and beyond
“as-intended” levels.
New and existing buildings can be commissioned, but en-
ergy savings from commissioning may not persist over time
as new problems arise due to equipment aging, changes in
use, alteration in control settings, etc. For example, a study
of 52 economizers at 22 sites that had been commissioned
when built found that 29 had stopped working after “a year
or two.”1 However, it appears that hardware-based commis-
sioning measures persist more often than controls-based
measures.2,3
Ongoing commissioning4 addresses the challenge of en-
suring that energy savings from commissioning persist by
enabling organizations to effectively diagnose problems as
they arise and to ensure that building equipment and systems
continue to perform at a high level under current building
conditions and requirements.
In this process, the party responsible for performing on-
going commissioning acquires data from building systems
and equipment. Often, these data come from the building’s
building automation system (BAS), as well as utility con-
sumption data (electric, gas, steam, chilled water, etc.) and
electricity consumption data from addressable switchgear,
but some may also come from data loggers.4,5,6,7 Next, the
data are analyzed to identify opportunities for performance
improvements and energy cost savings by comparing actual
performance to expected performance. Data analysis is au-
tomated or aided by software-based data visualization and/or
fault detection, diagnostics and optimization (FDDO) tools
of varying sophistication.
The ongoing commissioning provider delivers to the build-
ing operator a list of opportunities and recommendations to
reduce energy expenditures, often prioritized by energy cost
impact. These recommendations include controls, mainte-
nance, and hardware recommendations. Implementations
tend to focus on control changes because controls issues
often account for a significant portion of performance degra-
dation and usually can be addressed at little cost. Ultimately,
the building operator selects what changes he wants on-site
staff, the ongoing commissioning provider (OCP), or a third
party to implement.
Ongoing commissioning can be provided in at least three
ways. In many cases, building operators lack the time and
skills to detect and diagnose energy performance problems
and improvement opportunities. The first two approaches
outsource ongoing commissioning to experts with the tools,
time, and understanding to effectively manage building
energy performance.
1. A building owner enters into a contract with an OCP
who performs initial commissioning, with regular (e.g.,
quarterly) follow-up monitoring to assess building energy
performance and, as needed, additional on-site visits to
recommission building equipment and systems.4
2. A building owner enters into a contract with an OCP who
continuously receives building operations data at a remote
location. Off-site, the OCP uses FDDO tools to monitor
and evaluate energy performance and make recommenda-
tions for reducing energy cost at regular intervals (e.g.,
monthly).5
3. A building operator purchases a FDDO tool and imple-
ments the tool with help from the tool developer. The tool
continuously collects and analyzes the data to help the
operator identify opportunities to improve energy perfor-
mance; the building operations staff consults the tool as
often as they wish.7
Energy Savings Potential
Ongoing commissioning achieves energy savings in two
ways: first, from the initial commissioning of the building,
i.e., akin to retrocommissioning with potential additional
savings from optimization of building performance, and,
second, from maintaining those savings over time. The actual
energy savings potential from retrocommissioning a given
building varies greatly and depends on the types of systems in
place, how well the building operators maintain it, and what
failures occur. A significant volume of literature suggests that
By Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE;
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2008. ©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating,
Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be
copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form
without permission of ASHRAE.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
March 2008
ASHRAE Journal
69
retrocommissioning typically reduces
whole-building energy consumption by
5% to 20%, with an average of about
15%.8 Higher values (up to 30%) may
occur in some buildings.8 – 16
It is not clear how applicable these
savings estimates are to the overall
population of commercial buildings.
A large portion of commissioning case
studies focuses on larger buildings, i.e.,
those of more than 100,000 ft2 (9300
m2). These buildings are much more
likely to have central HVAC systems,
particularly ventilation systems that
frequently exhibit suboptimal op-
erations.8 In contrast, simpler unitary
systems account for more than half
of commercial building HVAC en-
ergy consumption and are common in
smaller buildings. Furthermore, many
buildings are selected for retrocom-
missioning because they consumed
high levels of energy and/or occupant
comfort complaints.17
The energy savings of ongoing com-
missioning relative to retrocommission-
ing are not well understood. Ongoing
commissioning saves additional energy
by providing the opportunity to refine
building operations over time based on
current (not initial) building conditions
and requirements, addressing any per-
formance issues that arise after initial
commissioning, and ensuring that the
energy savings of commissioning per-
sist over time. There are few publicly
available quantitative analyses of the
persistence of energy savings from
commissioning. Overall, they suggest
that many buildings and building sys-
tems maintain energy savings, while
a smaller portion see energy savings
degrade by several percent (of savings)
a year.3,18,19
Market Factors
Ongoing commissioning shares
several non-energy benefits of retro-
commissioning. By ensuring proper
function of building systems, com-
missioning improves climate control,
which reduces complaints and increases
occupant comfort. It also helps to avoid
indoor air quality problems that can
lead to occupant discomfort and liabil-
ity concerns. By diagnosing problems
prior to failure, ongoing commissioning
can also lower maintenance expenses,
help to prioritize maintenance activi-
ties, and reduce the risk of failures and
resulting downtime.4,8,20,21,22 Although
non-energy benefits are difficult to
quantify, data from a meta study of
commissioning suggest that building
operators perceive the non-energy value
of commissioning existing buildings
to be, on average, about two-thirds of
that from energy savings. Increased
equipment life, thermal comfort, and
IAQ ranked as the three most common
non-energy benefits in retrocommis-
sioning projects.8
Ongoing commissioning also enables
building operators to validate that
identified problems are addressed and
actually fixed.6
Few independent evaluations of the
payback of ongoing commissioning
exist. Two ongoing commissioning pro-
viders estimate average payback periods
of about one year for projects that they
carry out.4,6 This is similar to mean
payback period estimated for retrocom-
missioning.8,17 However, it is likely that
a one-year payback would not be typical
for all commercial buildings. Specifi-
cally, the cost of ongoing commissioning
does not scale linearly with the number
of measurement points, i.e., some costs
are relatively fixed. Consequently, larger
buildings (or multiple buildings, e.g., a
campus) with higher energy costs tend
to have more favorable economics than
smaller buildings or buildings with low
energy costs. In addition, ongoing com-
missioning tends to yield higher returns
for more sophisticated central plant
systems than for more basic physical
plants because the higher flexibility of
sophisticated systems allows greater
energy performance optimization.
Despite the many benefits of com-
missioning, the frequency of building
commissioning of any kind appears to be
low. Estimates from a decade ago found
that commercial building rates were less
than 5% for new buildings and less than
0.03% for existing buildings in a given
year.23 Ongoing commission rates are
likely to be even smaller.15
Advertisement formerly in this space.
70
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
Several reasons exist for the low
commissioning rates. A general lack of
awareness of commissioning—not just
ongoing commissioning—is a major
barrier. One OCP notes that most build-
ing owners are not familiar with the
benefits of ongoing commissioning.24
In addition, researchers found sig-
nificant variations in the actual energy
savings achieved from retrocommis-
sioning of different buildings. Ongoing
commissioning faces this same issue,
which makes its payback uncertain
at the beginning of a project. This, in
turn, increases owners’ perceived risk
of investing in ongoing commissioning
and decreases the likelihood that they
will do so.24 Finally, some building
operators may be reluctant to make
changes because they do not want to run
the risk that the changes will increase
complaint calls.15
Several technical challenges also inhibit
ongoing commissioning implementation,
formats can be challenging and time-in-
tensive. For more modern BASs, this can
often be overcome using communication
gateways, but that also increases cost.5
Data configuration, i.e., associating
data from the BAS with specific model
attributes in FDDO tools, is a labor-
intensive process that drives up the cost
of setting up ongoing commissioning.
Similarly, FDDO requires details about
equipment and system design and
performance characteristics. Often, de-
veloping and entering this information
also consumes significant amounts of
time, as it requires surveying buildings
and reviewing equipment and system
documentation, followed by manual
entering of system data.24
Overall, ongoing commissioning
is more attractive for larger buildings
or campuses with a modern BAS. For
smaller buildings with unitary HVAC,
unitary equipment with onboard FDD
would likely be a more cost-effective op-
tion than ongoing commissioning once it
becomes established in the market.25

driving up its cost. The OCP must be able

that it represents a deal-breaker because
implementing… would require a major
controls system upgrade.”24 Buildings that
lack direct digital control (DDC) are usu-
ally not promising candidates for ongoing
commissioning for the same reason.6

About the Authors
Photo 1: St. Michael’s Hospital (SMH), Toronto, conducted a full facility condition assesment program led by a third-party review team.
O&M Cost Management
For Health-Care Facility
F
 aced with difficult annual funding decisions, hospital admin-
istrators routinely must sift through many competing capital
By Ron Saporta; Robyn Ellis, Student Member ASHRAE; & Michael Kwok, P.Eng.
funding requests. Often facility requests, not covered by the annual
operations and maintenance (O&M) budget (such as large HVAC
equipment replacements), lose out to competing clinical require-
ments (such as adding an MRI machine).
perspective: in this case a large modern
teaching hospital set in an aging urban
facility, St. Michael’s Hospital (SMH). As
one of only two trauma centers in Toron-
to, SMH spans approximately 1.5 million
ft2 (139 354 m2), including four intensive
care units, 24 operating rooms, and many
laboratories and outpatient clinics. SMH
provides primary through quaternary care
How can hospitals better delineate facility
infrastructure funding from other capital
funding requests? How can hospitals posi-
tion facility renewal programs to better
serve O&M needs and intelligently plan
for the changing condition of the facility?
What metrics are available to indicate where
priority funding ought to be placed?
This study addresses facility infra-
structure renewal from the public sector
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2008. ©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2008
ASHRAE Journal
35
allocated (in addition to base funding) approximately $0.10
to $0.12/ft2 ($1.08/m2 to $1.29/m2) per year or 0.02% of the
hospital’s facility replacement value as part of a program called
HIRF (Hospital Infrastructure Renewal Funding). Although it is
a step in the right direction, this funding proves insufficient as
an exclusive source of renewal funding. It falls noticeably short
of industry averages identified by the Association of Higher
Education Facilities Officers (APPA).
APPA indicates, in a U.S.-based study of educational facili-
ties, that the public sector average spending on renewal projects
is 1.681% of facility replacement value annually for public
facilities and 0.944% for private facilities.3 Also, a report pub-
lished by the International Facility Management Association
(IFMA) predicts a facility deterioration rate of 2.5% annual
for a well-maintained facility.1 This is an area of continued
industry benchmarking, where goals are largely set according to
the needs of individual facilities rather than relying on industry
absolutes. Infrastructure planning, including benchmarking
and software management, and extending well beyond simple
preventive maintenance scheduling, is a relatively recent and
undocumented movement in facility management practice.
How did SMH come to manage building infrastructure re-
quirements separately from other capital funding needs? The
hospital conducted a full facility condition assessment program
(FCAP) in December 2006 through January 2007, which was
led by an independent third-party review of architectural, me-
chanical, electrical, and security building infrastructure. Two
important components exist for a successful FCAP. The first in-
volves establishing the correct level of assessments required by
the establishment and assigning appropriately skilled resources
to the project. The second is using a centralized application to
store all of the information and analyze the data.
During the Facility Condition Assessment
It is generally agreed that a detailed, non-destructive physi-
cal assessment as prescribed by the ASTM International and
the National Research Council Canada is sufficient to pro-
vide the information necessary to guide decision-making
processes. In practice, this requires a team of three assessors
with backgrounds in architecture, mechanical engineering
and electrical engineering to assess the building for issues
such as end-of-service-life, code compliance, accessibility,
and other concerns. The information collected is analyzed,
to a diverse patient population, including services in cardiovas-
cular surgery, neurosurgery, oncology, diabetes comprehensive
care, inner-city health care, and therapeutic endoscopy.
Modern Facility Challenges in a Hospital
The case study at SMH echoes what many hospitals face with
similar infrastructure challenges. O&M departments in hospi-
tals and facilities are not always able to address infrastructure
challenges and take on large maintenance projects. One reason
is maintenance projects require capital funding starting at about
$5,000 to $10,000 per project.1 However, experience at hospitals
shows that the cost of large infrastructure renewal projects can
range from 10 to 100 times those figures. Unlike O&M fund-
ing, which is managed internally, requests for capital funding
compete publicly from the same limited funding pool, whether
they are requests for facility infrastructure replacements or for
new clinical equipment.
The obvious reason why large annual facility infrastructure
projects are needed is the ongoing deterioration of aging build-
ing systems. Frequently, a combination of factors contribute to
the obsolescence of facility infrastructure:
Changing health-care delivery needs
••
2 often lead to
functional changes of space (e.g., conversion of offices
to labs);
Changes in codes and standards (e.g., ASHRAE, Cana-
••
dian Standards Association);
Increases in occupancy lead to insufficiency of original
••
design capacity;
Replacement of process equipment; and
••
Increasing concern for energy conservation and
••
sustainability.
Examples of projects to address the latter requirements are
conversion of fans to variable speed drives or replacement of
domestic water-cooled equipment with modern air-cooled
systems.
Impact on O&M
Caught in the struggle to address these challenges without
adequate programs for infrastructure replacement, O&M
departments are stretched thin dealing with daily HVAC (and
other) capacity shortages and infrastructure-related backlogs.
Resources are tied up in unplanned projects, system failures,
shutdowns, system isolations, and rebuilds. Band-Aid fixes are
used to keep old systems running longer than advisable from
an economic or reliability standpoint. Quick fixes don’t address
equipment deterioration, nor do they account for issues of ob-
solescence such as the need for increased capacity or changes
to industry codes.
Before the Facility Condition Assessment
Prior to the facility assessment, the hospital had a well-
developed strategic planning process but no high-level metric
to quantitatively describe the current state of facility infra-
structure needs. The government funding body, recognizing
annual health-care infrastructure renewal needs of the province,
Unlike O&M funding, which is managed inter-
nally, requests for capital funding compete pub-
licly from the same limited funding pool, whether
they are requests for facility infrastructure
replacements or for new clinical equipment.

ASHRAE Journal

prioritized, and categorized so that further data analysis is
possible at any level. The assessed need is also priced so that
the facility condition index (FCI) can be calculated and used
for comparative purposes.
All information that is gathered and analyzed should be
input into a common database for all sites and departments.
This helps improve communications and prevent the isolation
of information effectively created when multiple databases
existed. A Web-based application is beneficial because dis-
tributed workforces can collaborate with each other on one
dataset. With current information on facility needs, the point
where each system lies in its life cycle, and the costs of the
required work, it is possible to calculate the FCI and begin
to use this in building funding scenarios. These scenarios are
able to show the impact of specific funding streams on the
condition of the facilities and help managers prioritize and
manage their needs within the facility.
FCAP Results
The main findings of the FCAP at the hospital are quanti-
fied through a database containing FCI information, costs of
infrastructure requirements and system renewals, as well as
projections of infrastructure needs up to 20 years into the fu-
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
38
ASHRAE Journal
M a r c h 2 0 0 8
Figure 3: Funding and FCI projections based on funding scenarios:
past, current and goal.
ture. The capital planning and management solutions process
conducted by the consultants is shown in Figure 1. This process
has given SMH detailed and dynamic economic data and tools
to promote, benchmark, and manage renewal funding. Using
this mechanism, annual renewal funding successfully has
been requested separately from other types of capital funding
requests. This funding is dedicated solely to maintaining or
improving the condition of the facility’s infrastructure and ad-
dressing facility obsolescence.
Facility Condition Index (FCI)
FCI is a type of current state analysis that has become an
industry standard and is defined by an IFMA report as “a
comparative metric to help monitor degradation rates,” 1 spe-
cifically as follows:
1
Deferred Maintenance + Capital Renewal
Facility Condition Index =
Current Replacement Value
Both the deferred maintenance (DM) and capital renewal (CR)
values were dollar value totals determined during the facility as-
sessment. Together, they represent the funding required beyond
the annual O&M budget to maintain the facility in a useable,
reliable condition, in compliance with all necessary codes. The
larger the FCI is, the poorer the condition of the facility. The hos-
pital has a unique mix of old and new hospital wings constructed
from 1910 to 2002, which translates into assets ranging widely in
FCI values. It is commonly recognized that a fair to good facility
typically has an FCI of 10% to 15 % or less.4 On average as a
facility, SMH is close to this desirable range at present, but what
about the future?
Intelligent Facility Condition Planning
How can infrastructure renewal funding be positioned within
the capital funding context to better serve larger facility O&M
needs and intelligently plan for the changing condition of the
facility? Beyond providing facility condition metrics, FCAP

March 2008
ASHRAE Journal
41
results were organized in a commercially available software
tool that gave the hospital the ability to prioritize and forecast
infrastructure requirements.
Priority 1 requirements are identified as requirements that
should be addressed in the first year after the study, whereas
Priorities 2 to 5 have suggested completion in two to five years.
Typical examples of Priority 1 requirements are those that di-
rectly affect life safety, code requirements, system reliability,
and equipment that is beyond its useful life. Priority 2 require-
ments are those that are likely to become Priority 1 require-
ments, approximately one year into the future. The breakdown
of findings by priority at the hospital is shown in Figure 2.
One can see how useful this type of software can be in terms
of organizing, prioritizing and communicating facility renewal
information, but the achievements go beyond this.
After the Facility Condition Assessment (FCAP)
Using the FCAP tools, the hospital was able to establish and
promote dynamic funding goals through forecasting. For ex-
ample, at the outset of the assessment, the hospital established
a renewal funding goal of approximately 0.5% of the facility
replacement value, with a predicted injection of funding in
2011 corresponding to a forecasted result of reducing the
FCI from 0.16 down to 0.15 by 2013. This proposal was well
supported through detailed reports and forecasting thanks to
FCAP software and well positioned as part of a comprehen-
sive plan for infrastructure renewal to hospital management.
After the first year of this pilot program, the resulting re-
newal funding allocation increase was a mixed success as it
came to only 0.1% of facility replacement value. While this
represents approximately a 500% improvement over previous
years, the FCI projections for future years have only marginally
improved. The resulting pilot project funding was five times
lower than the forecasted funding based on 0.5% of facility
replacement value. This information is summarized in a series
of forecasts shown in Figure 3.
Summary of Successes
The successes associated with establishing a comprehensive
infrastructure renewal program at the hospital are mixed and
ongoing as the mechanism slowly develops momentum. In
less than a year since the release of FCAP results, infrastruc-
ture renewal funding has increased by 500%. Perhaps a more
significant success is the development of a metric that can be
used to justify to hospital management further spending on
infrastructure. This is an analysis tool showing different funding
scenarios for development of intelligent facility plans. This tool
has raised awareness in senior management of the importance
of infrastructure funding and allowed the facility to establish
short-term and long-term funding goals.
Operations and maintenance and corporate planning are able
to anticipate future costs better, prioritize and monitor build-
ing condition (FCI) and report on progress. In this way, annual
renewal funding has been successfully delineated from other
types of capital funding requests at the hospital. Infrastructure
renewal funding is dedicated to maintaining or improving the
condition of the facility’s infrastructure and addressing facility
obsolescence, therefore reducing some of the stresses on O&M
resources. �����������������������������������������������
Now, a mechanism is in place for quickly recom-
mending actions to solve facility challenges and providing cost
estimates to address present and future facility requirements.
Facility infrastructure planning tools and programs are rela-
tively new for facility managers and the availability of indus-
trywide benchmarks in this area is limited. As knowledge and
historical data in FCAP increases (e.g., spending comparisons
across hundreds of similar facilities) there may be opportunities
to better make the case for large O&M or facility infrastructure
projects in the future.

files/PDFs/FCDSWebReport\_0405.pdf.
4. IFMA, et al. “Asset Lifecycle Model for Total Cost of Ownership
