Table 2: Cooling capacity, cost premiums and simple paybacks by climate.
the annual utility costs are actually higher in all climates except
for Miami for the demand rate assumed in this example.
Table 2 shows the peak cooling capacity of the air-condition-
ing units by climate location. The cost premium for moving
from an EER of 9.2 to and EER of 10.6 is estimated as $400
per ton per unit of EER increase. A simple payback is calculated
for the efficiency enhancement for each climate location. The
project committee uses a discounted life-cycle payback rather
than a simple payback, so the payback values shown here are
not necessarily reflective of values that might be used by the
project committee. However, they do give an indication of the
relative results that would be expected when the discounted
life-cycle paybacks are applied. The simple paybacks for the
demand rate are always shorter than the simple paybacks for the
flat rate, and the differences between the two grow substantially
larger as you move to colder climates.
Payback periods are more sensitive to climate variations
for the flat rate as shown in Figure 1. The payback period for
the flat rate increases by a factor of 3.7 to 1 as you move from
Miami to Minneapolis. This reflects the fact that in the colder
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
18
ASHRAE Journal
A p r i l 2 0 0 8
climates the need for cooling, particularly in non-summer
months, is substantially reduced, so higher efficiency cooling
equipment is less cost effective. However, under a demand rate,
the payback periods vary less than they do under the flat rate.
The reason for this is that the buildings in the colder climates
operate at a lower load factor and for approximately seven
months of the year the demand ratchet is triggered. Triggering
the ratchet raises the cost of the electric bills for over half the
year, which tends to keep the payback periods more consistent
across all climates.
Using construction activity data from F.W. Dodge for
1999 – 2005, the payback periods in Table 2 can be combined to
arrive at an overall weighted payback period across climates.3
When this is done the weighted payback period for the flat rate is
roughly 37 years versus roughly 20 years for the demand rate. This
data shows that paybacks are roughly half as long for our example
case when the demand rate is used. The implication of this finding
is that for equipment or measures that contribute substantially to
peak demand the use of a demand rate when establishing minimum
efficiency criteria likely will justify higher levels of efficiency.
Criteria Versus Compliance
Utility rates are used in two contexts in the standard. The first
context is that of establishing the mandatory and prescriptive
criteria for the standard. In the past the project committee has
done this for electricity use by applying a life-cycle discounted
payback using a weighted average electricity consumption cost
(in other words, a flat rate) for the U.S. As noted previously, this
weighted average electricity cost did include some of the effects
of demand since it represented the total electric bill divided by
the total consumption. It was not a true demand rate; however,
it was certainly easier to apply and, perhaps, defend. The second
context of utility rates in the standard is when a designer or
owner decides to achieve compliance with the standard through
the use of the Energy Cost Budget (ECB) compliance approach
contained in Section 11 of the standard.
When a design professional elects to use the standard’s ECB
compliance path, the annual energy cost of a budget building
design is compared to the annual energy cost of a proposed
building design. To comply, the proposed building design must
have an energy cost that is not higher than that of the budget
building design.
The budget building and proposed building designs must
have identical conditioned floor areas, exterior dimensions and
orientations. The budget building design’s building envelope
and mechanical/electrical systems must meet the prescriptive
requirements of the standard. However, although the pro-
posed building design must meet the standard’s mandatory
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
20
ASHRAE Journal
A p r i l 2 0 0 8
requirements, it does not have to meet
the prescriptive requirements—with the
proviso that the overall design must have
an annual energy cost that does not ex-
ceed that of the budget building. In other
words, design features that do not meet
the standard’s prescriptive requirements
must be offset by other design features
that, in terms of energy cost, exceed the
standard’s prescriptive requirements for
those features.
The energy cost comparison is achieved
by running energy simulations for the
budget and proposed building designs,
using the rules set out in Section 11 of
Standard 90.1. Among other things, these
rules stipulate that both simulations must
use the same climatic data and the same
purchased energy rates.
For ECB purposes, Standard 90.1 does
not mandate the use of the national aver-
age energy rates. Instead, it stipulates
that the purchased energy rates shall be
those “approved by the adopting author-
ity, defined as the agent or agency that
adopts the standard (e.g., state or local
code agency.” Three major reasons exist
for this. First, it allows the use of rates
applicable to the local area. They may
be representative of the area, or they
may be the rates that are applicable to the
specific project, at the adopting author-
ity’s discretion.
Second, for electrical rates, it allows
for the inclusion of separate consumption
and demand components in the overall
rate. Therefore, an incentive exists for the
designer to include lower lighting power
density and HVAC systems with low
electrical demand in a proposed design
and further improved envelope designs as
lower demand charges will reduce annual
energy cost.
Third, it permits the use of energy rates
that are as close as possible, and may
be identical to, the energy rates design
professionals need to use in evaluating
alternative designs or systems from a
project economic optimization perspec-
tive, rather than from an energy standard
compliance perspective.
It could be argued that allowing the
inclusion of demand charges in ECB
simulations could result in a proposed
design with a reduced electrical demand
that complies with the standard, even if
its overall energy consumption exceeded
that of the budget building design.
This could occur, but there is a wider
perspective at play. The summation of
electrical demand from all buildings in
an electrical service area determines the
generating, transmission, and distribu-
tion capacity that must be provided by
electrical utilities serving the area. To
the extent that buildings have lower
peak demand, fewer resources need to
be applied to construct and operate the
electrical system infrastructure.
Conclusions
The simple example shown indicates
that the use of a demand rate for setting
criteria could result in higher levels of ef-
ficiency being selected by the 90.1 project
committee for the prescriptive criteria for
the 2010 version of Standard 90.1. This,
of course, suggests that perhaps the com-
mittee should use demand rates, since
their use might assist in reaching the goal
of a 30% energy savings over Standard
90.1-2004. As pointed out previously
the committee would need to establish a
credible demand rate that could be used
for all of its analysis, and this could be
difficult to accomplish.
Assuming that a credible demand
rate can be established, the committee
could then consider applying it to those
measures that contribute substantially to
peak demand. However, doing so would
increase the already considerable cri-
teria evaluation demands on the project
committee. Additional resources must
be made available for this additional
work. Re-evaluating all mandatory and
prescriptive criteria for a demand rate
is probably not feasible for Standard
90.1-2010, but could be completed in
the course of its continuous maintenance
revisions.
Alternatively, the committee could
calculate a new “effective” consumption
rate that embodies, to a larger extent than
at present, the effect of demand. This rate
will be higher than the weighted aver-
age rate that the committee otherwise
would use, which would have the effect
of helping to cost justify higher levels
of efficiency. In either case, the project
committee should take a serious look
at demand rates for electricity in for-
mulating the requirements for Standard
90.1-2010.

About the Authors
R
ecently, I was involved with a chiller plant retrofit proj-
ect in which we provided a substantial energy-efficiency
incentive to the owner. Part of the incentive process is to
perform pre- and post-measurement and verification (M&V) to
validate the initial energy savings calculations. We installed a
data acquisition system, power meter, flow meter and matched
temperature sensors. As part of the project scope, the owner
also required that the existing building energy management
system (EMS) monitor and trend ongoing performance of the
entire plant throughout the term of the loan used to pay for
the equipment upgrades (10-year loan).
Monitoring
Chiller Plant
Performance
The controls contractor installed a
completely separate system: another flow
meter, two temperature sensors, and pulled
the power off the new chiller control panel.
The following is a presentation of the two
systems and corresponding results.
System Installed by M&V Team
As part of the incentive process, the
nonprofit M&V team installed the fol-
lowing equipment (Figure 1):
Class 2000 power meter. Accu-

C12.16;
Chilled water supply and return
••
temperature sensors: solid-state,
integrated circuit temperature
sensors, ±0.15°F (±0.08°C)

Observations
The Southern California campus central plant suffered from
significant mixing (low ∆T), inefficient constant speed opera-
tion, and low-load instability. As part of an energy-efficiency
project, the 750-ton (2638 kW) plant was converted into a
primary-only, all-variable speed system with individual vari-
able speed booster pumps in each of the campus buildings. The
retrofitted plant consisted of two variable speed chillers with
variable speed condenser pumps, tower fans, and chilled water
pumps. All remaining three-way control valves were converted
to two-way direct digital control.
A controls schematic (Figure 1) details the central plant and
location of the measurement instruments. The results from the
two separate monitoring systems were plotted for four days from
Sept. 7 to Sept. 11, 2006, during which only the 450 ton (1583
kW) chiller operated. Figures 2, 3 and 4 show chilled water flow
(gpm), chilled water ∆T in degrees Fahrenheit (Treturn – Tsupply),
and chiller power (kW) respectively. Average data was recorded
and stored every 15 minutes in the M&V system versus every
10 minutes for the energy management system (EMS).

Figure 2: Chilled water flow.
Chiller flow rate (Figure 2). Chilled
water flow varied from 220 gpm to 613
gpm (13.9 L/s to 38.7 L/s). Two identical
dual turbine flow meters were installed;
the only differences were the diameter of
the chilled water piping and the length
of the straight run to each flow meter.\*
In Figure 2, chilled water flow varied
slightly between the two trends. The vari-
ance is the result of the difference in re-
cording interval frequencies and irregular
flow conditions (swirl) caused by a short
straight pipe run in the M&V team’s flow
meter. Based on the trended data the two
flows differed by 3.9% (average).
Chiller ∆T (Figure 3). Chilled water
∆T varied from 3.4°F to 12.9°F (1.9°C to
7.2°C).† The ∆T shows the most lopsided
readings of the four figures. The EMS
temperature sensors were not calibrated
at the site resulting in unmatched values
and higher chiller efficiency errors.‡
Chiller power (Figure 4). The chiller
drew between 15 kW and 170 kW. Power
varied slightly between the two trends and
can be calculated at 4.2% (average dif-
ference). The variance is the result of the
difference in recording interval frequen-
cies, data acquisition accuracies, as well
as resolution due to harmonics from the
compressor variable frequency drive.
Efficiency of the chiller in kW/ton ver-
sus load (from August through October
2006) is plotted in Figure 5 using the
following equation:
tonnage = (gpm)(∆T)/24
A third “adjusted” chiller efficiency
is also plotted; the EMS temperature
sensors were mismatched by an average
of 1.8°F (1°C). The 1.8°F (1°C) discrep-
ancy was determined based on trended
supply and return temperatures when
the chiller plant was shut down (because
of low outdoor air temperatures). Dur-
ing these three consecutive days in late
October, all temperature sensors came
to equilibrium, and the discrepancy was
logged. Adding this 1.8°F (1°C) tem-
perature correction for the entire EMS
trended period creates the “adjusted”
efficiency curve.§ The original estimate
and trended average results for the three-
month period are as follows:

tions, for a distance of 10 pipe diameters upstream and five pipe diameters downstream.

chillers, capacity is reduced by the lower ∆T across the bundle.

still used by loads on the campus.

Conclusions
M&V is always a delicate balance
between cost effectiveness and accuracy.
The general rule of thumb is that M&V
should be less than 10% of the total
incentive allocation.\*\* In most applica-
tions, we save cost by solely relying on
the building energy management system
for data acquisition. But, in this case,
new technologies warranted the extra
cost to get accurate before-and-after
performance data. The data was used for
education, experience and for estimat-
ing future energy projects using the new
technologies.
We learned from each central plant
M&V experience that matched, high ac-
curacy temperature sensors are the most
\*\*This rule-of-thumb was developed from the Measured Savings Approach of the Standard Performance
Contract program administered by California’s investor-owned utilities.
†† Pacific Gas & Electric monitoring protocols. Source: Kenneth Gillespie. Note: ASHRAE Guideline 22P calls for
power measurement devices capable of sensing fundamental harmonics through the 33rd (odd and even).
important piece of the puzzle. A one or
two degree Fahrenheit error in ∆T can
throw the tonnage calculation at low loads
off by as much as 25% to 50%. The 56%
differences in sensor accuracy (±0.34°F
versus ±0.15°F) also contribute to less
accuracy. Based on the logged data, the
EMS temperature sensors provided a
19.7% average error for the trended pe-
riod. Also, we have learned that variable
frequency drives on equipment call for
higher quality power meters capable of
sensing direct current and fundamental
harmonic through the 50th harmonic (odd
and even) with no derating in accuracy
through the 25th harmonic.††
Extrapolating the data using a re-
gression analysis, the project exceeded
expectations for energy savings and the
full incentive was paid to the customer.
Conservative energy calculations coupled
with Web-based pre- and post-monitoring
resulted in a successful energy project,
lower energy bills, and happy owners.

Steve Greenberg, P.E., is energy management
engineer at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
in Berkeley, Calif. Dale Sartor, P.E., is applica-
tions team leader at Lawrence Berkeley National
Laboratory in Berkeley, Calif. Peter Rumsey, P.E.,
is principal at Rumsey Engineers in Oakland, Calif.
John Weale, P.E., is senior engineer at Rumsey
Engineers in Oakland, Calif.
About the Authors
Metrics for Energy Efficiency
By Paul Mathew, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE; Steve Greenberg, P.E.; Dale Sartor, P.E.;
Peter Rumsey, P.E., Member ASHRAE; and John Weale, P.E.
L
aboratory owners have explicit goals for energy efficiency in their
 facilities. For example, the Energy Policy Act of 2005 (EPACT
2005) requires all new federal buildings to exceed ANSI/ASHRAE/
IESNA Standard 90.1-2004, Energy Standard for Buildings Except
Low-Rise Residential Buildings, by at least 30%. A new labora-
tory is more likely to meet energy-efficiency goals if quantitative
metrics and targets are specified in programming documents and
then tracked during the course of the delivery process. If not, any
additional capital costs or design time associated with attaining
higher efficiencies can be difficult to justify.
This article describes key energy-
efficiency metrics and benchmarks for
laboratories that have been developed and
applied to several laboratory buildings for
their design and operation. In addition
to traditional whole building energy use
metrics (e.g., Btu/ft2·yr [kWh/m2·yr]),
the article describes HVAC system met-
rics (e.g., ventilation W/cfm [W/L·s–1]),
which can be used to identify the presence
or absence of energy features and oppor-
tunities during design and operation.
Whole Building Metrics
Applying Standard 90.1-2004 to Labs
Standard 90.1-2004 is increasingly
being used as a benchmark for new con-
Laboratory Performance
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2008. ©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.

ASHRAE Journal
41
struction, especially those projects
seeking a LEED® rating. Typically,
this involves setting goals relative to
the performance of a baseline build-
ing, as defined in the standard. How-
ever, in practice, simply specifying
a goal of “x% better than Standard
90.1-2004” is inadequate for labo-
ratories; it leaves several key factors
open to interpretation, which in turn
affect the meaning of the percentage
reduction goals.
The Laboratories for the 21st Cen-
tury Program (Labs21) has developed
modeling guidelines,1 which clarify or
modify selected sections of Standard
90.1 to make them more applicable to
systems serving laboratory spaces, as
summarized in Table 1. Although the
Labs21 guidelines are designed to be
used in conjunction with Appendix G
of the standard, they were developed
by Labs21 and are not officially part
of the standard. However, it is anticipated that most of the key
provisions will be incorporated into the standard through the
continuous maintenance process. The fan power limitation
has been largely addressed through Addendum ac, which was
incorporated into the 2007 version of the standard. To the
extent that other elements in the guidelines are not yet part of
the standard, it is recommended that they be followed when
modeling laboratory buildings.
Basis for percent reduction. Two ways commonly used to
express percentage reduction are:
Percentage reduction relative to total loads (including
••
process loads); and
Percentage reduction relative to regulated loads (exclud-
••
ing process loads).
Appendix G of the 2004 version specifies the first approach
(i.e., based on total loads). Earlier versions of LEED-NC
(prior to 2.2) followed the second approach. This often created
confusion about what was included or excluded in the percent-
age calculation, and was especially problematic in laboratory
buildings. For example, fume hoods were sometimes included
because they are part of the HVAC system, and other times
excluded because they were considered a process load. Figure
1 compares different options for calculating percentage reduc-
tion for the Science and Technology Facility at the National
Renewable Energy Laboratory, which received a LEED-NC
Platinum rating. The difference between the options underscores
the need to clearly define how it is calculated and compared
with other facilities.2
Although percentage reduction of total load is the primary
metric that should be used, it is also useful to track percentage
reduction of regulated loads, since it provides a measure of the
efficiency of features that designers have significant control

Clarify that a baseline building must have
either a VAV system or energy recovery,
but not both. This provision applies to all
laboratory air-handling systems, not just
systems serving fume hoods.
II. Laboratory Fan
Power Limitation
6.5.3.1 Fan Power Limitation
G3.1.2.9 Fan Power
Increase the allowable fan power limita-
tions. Although the standard provides
pressure credits for filtering systems,
heat recovery, etc., laboratory fan sys-
tems typically exceed the fan limitations
even with these credits.
III. Modeling Load
Diversity and

Impacts

(new) G3.1.3.16 Supply-Air-

Ensure that reheat energy use due to in-
ternal equipment load variations is prop-
erly modeled. Labs have large variations
of internal equipment loads from one
space to the next—this has a substantial
impact on reheat energy use.
Table 1: Issues addressed by the Labs21 modeling guidelines.
over. This is particularly true in laboratories, where process
loads can vary significantly across different projects, and design
estimates are often grossly inaccurate.
Metrics Based on Empirical Performance
Although metrics and benchmarks based on Standard 90.1 are
useful for exploring design alternatives, many owners and de-
signers are uncomfortable with the wide variability in modeling
results. Therefore, whole building targets should be evaluated
against empirical benchmarks that are based on actual measured
energy use of comparable buildings. For example, the energy
goals for a new laboratory at Lawrence Berkeley National Labo-
ratory were benchmarked against other laboratories (Figure 2)
with similar climatic context and lab area ratio (ratio of area
requiring 100% outside air to gross building area).
Whole building metrics, discussed previously, are useful in as-
sessing the overall efficiency level for a building. The remainder of
this article describes HVAC system level metrics that can be used
to identify specific opportunities for efficiency improvement.
Ventilation Metrics
Minimum Required Ventilation Rate
Ventilation dominates energy use in most laboratories,
especially chemical and biological laboratories. One of the
key drivers of ventilation energy use is the minimum ventila-
tion rate required for health and safety. The only exceptions
to this are laboratories where the air change rates are driven
by thermal loads (and always exceed minimum ventilation
rates for health and safety) or where high fume hood density,
typically greater than 1 ft2 (0.09 m2) of hood work surface per
25 ft2 (2.32 m2) of laboratory, drives the minimum flow. The
purpose of benchmarking minimum ventilation rates is to ex-

plore opportunities for optimization, i.e., reducing air change
rates while maintaining or improving safety. Air change rates
should be benchmarked with two metrics:
Air changes per hour (ACH). This is the most commonly
used metric. Various standards and guidelines indicate that this
can vary between 4 and 12 (Table 2), which is a wide range.
Values higher than 6 ACH (when occupied) and 4 ACH (unoc-
cupied) may represent opportunities for optimization, or else
should be justified as being required for health and safety.
cfm/ft2 (L/s·m2). Some laboratory professionals believe that
this is a more appropriate metric, given that laboratory hazards
are more related to floor area than volume, i.e., a laboratory with
a high ceiling does not necessarily require more ventilation. The
International Building Code (2003) requires a rate of 1 cfm/ft2
(5 L/s · m2) for H-5 hazard environments. Here again, values
higher than this may represent opportunities for optimization.
Hood Density
Fume hoods are prodigious consumers of energy, and lab
planners should work with owners to carefully avoid installing
more and larger hoods than are necessary for programmatic
requirements. Specifically, fume hoods should not be used for
purposes that can be effectively met with lower-energy alter-
natives such as snorkels, balance hoods, and chemical storage
cabinets. It is recommended that fume hood density should be
benchmarked with other labs that have similar programmatic
requirements. For example, Figure 3 shows the range of fume
hood density (expressed as number of hoods/5,000 gross square
feet) in various laboratories in the University of California and
California State University campuses. Based on this chart,
values higher than about three hoods/5,000 gross square feet
(three hoods/465 m2) may present opportunities for optimizing
the number of fume hoods.

Fume Hood Sash Management
Once the number and size of fume
hoods has been optimized, the next ma-
jor opportunity is to reduce fume hood
energy use by reducing airflow through
low-volume fume hoods, and VAV hoods
with effective sash management. Although
no commonly used metrics exist for sash
management, we suggest using a metric
such as fume hood airflow management
ratio, defined as the ratio of the average
flow to the minimum flow. Minimum flow
is the flow through the fume hood when
the sash is closed. For a typical 6 ft (1.8
m) fume hood, this is usually about 300
cfm (142 L/s), which corresponds to the
NFPA – 45 mandated minimum of 25 cfm/
ft2 (125 L/s·m2) of work surface area. A
typical 6 ft (1.8 m) fume hood with an 18
in. (46 cm) sash-stop operates at about 900
cfm (425 L/s). Therefore, if the sash were
never closed, the airflow management ratio
would be 3. If the sash were closed 50%
of the time, the ratio would be 2.
Figure 4 shows the impact of sash man-
agement training on airflow management
ratios for a laboratory at Duke University,
indicating a significant improvement in
sash management as a result of the train-
ing and awareness campaign.
Ventilation Airflow Efficiency
Ventilation airflow efficiency is typi-
cally the most significant way that HVAC
design engineers can influence overall
lab efficiency. There are two key related
metrics:
System pressure drop, in. w.g. (Pa).
Each component in the supply and ex-
haust system can be optimized for low
pressure drop. Table 3 compares typical
practice with low-pressure drop design
for the Tahoe Center for Environmental
Studies, which received a LEED Plati-
num rating. (Additional information on
low-pressure drop benchmarks and design guidelines are de-
scribed by Weale et al.10 and Labs21.11)
Ventilation system, W/cfm (W/L · s –1). This metric is defined
as the total power of supply and exhaust fans divided by the total
cfm of supply and exhaust fans. It provides an overall measure of
how efficiently air is moved through the laboratory, from inlet to
exhaust, and takes into account low-pressure drop design as well
as fan system efficiency (motors, belts, drives). Figure 5 shows
the range of ventilation system efficiency at peak loads for various
laboratories in the Labs21 benchmarking database. There is a wide

(3.8 W/L · s –1). The fan power limitations specified in Standard
90.1-2004 provide an additional benchmark.
Special Considerations for Labs
Temperature and Humidity Setpoints
Temperature and humidity setpoints in laboratory spaces are
driven by human comfort and laboratory function (experimenta-
tion/equipment requirements). Laboratory users and planners
sometimes call for tight tolerances based on laboratory func-

45
tion, without evaluating whether
these are actually required. Tight
tolerances can increase energy use
due to reheat and humidification.
It is recommended that tolerances
tighter than those required for hu-
man comfort (e.g., based on ANSI/
ASHRAE Standard 55-2004, Ther-
mal Environmental Conditions for
Human Occupancy), be carefully
evaluated and explicitly justified.
At the global ecology center at
Stanford University, equipment
requiring tight tolerances (70±1°F
[21±0.5°C]) was grouped into a
dedicated area, so that other areas
of the lab could be controlled to
wider tolerances (73±5°F, 23±3°C)
with some rarely accessed freezers
and growth chambers actually re-
located to a minimally conditioned
adjacent structure controlled to
55°F to 95°F (13°C to 35°C).
Heating and Cooling System
Efficiency
The key metrics and benchmarks
to evaluate the efficiency of chiller and boiler systems in labs
are no different than those typically used in other commercial
buildings. These include chiller plant efficiency (kW/ton),
cooling load (tons/gsf [ton/m2]), boiler efficiency (%), pump-
ing efficiency (hp/gpm [W/L · s –1]), etc. Since these are well-
documented elsewhere, they are not discussed here. However,
two additional metrics that have special impact on lab efficiency
bear further discussion:
Chiller system minimum turndown ratio. Laboratory
systems are often oversized due to reliability/redundancy re-
quirements, overestimated process loads, or other factors. Even

have been 1.91.

when systems are right-sized, many hours occur when loads are
much lower than peak. Therefore, chiller systems in labs should
be designed for low minimum turndown ratios, defined as the
ratio of minimum load (with continuous compressor operation
without hot gas bypass or other false loading methods) to design
load. Standard practice would be about 20%. Good and better
practice benchmarks would be 10% and 5% respectively. In the
Molecular Foundry at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory,
the chiller system is capable of a 5% turndown ratio. In labs with
tight humidity control, even lower ratios are warranted, unless
alternative dehumidification strategies are adopted.

Laboratory Plug Loads: Design vs. Measured
Reheat energy use factor. Reheat energy use can be significant
in labs due to tight temperature and humidity requirements, wide
variation in loads served by a given air-handling system,12 or poorly
calibrated controls. Although no well-established metric exists for
assessing reheat energy use, we suggest a metric such as reheat
energy use factor, defined as the ratio of the reheat energy use to the
total space heating energy use. An alternative metric may be ratio of
reheat design capacity to chiller design capacity. The best practice
benchmark for this would be 0% (i.e., complete elimination of reheat
energy use for temperature control). The Koshland Integrated Natural
Science Center at Haverford College achieves this by using dual heat
wheels and separation of thermal and ventilation systems.13
Plug Load Metrics
Equipment loads in laboratories are frequently overestimated
because designers often use estimates based on nameplate
data, and design assumptions of high usage. This results in
oversized HVAC systems, increased initial construction costs,
and increased energy use due to inefficiencies at low part-load
operation.14 The following related metrics can be used to assess
and compare design and measured plug loads:
Laboratory design plug load W/ft2 (W/m2). The values
may vary across lab spaces in a given building. Note that the
assumption for electrical system design is usually higher than
that for HVAC system design.
Laboratory actual (measured) plug load W/ft2 (W/m2).
For a building currently in design, it is recommended that
measurements be taken in a comparable laboratory and those
data be used for sizing. This is obtained by taking continu-
ous measurements at the panel serving laboratory plug loads.
For HVAC system design, it is more appropriate to consider
the maximum of the 15-minute interval averages (rather than
maximum instantaneous load), since HVAC systems typically
do not react to the instantaneous loads.
Figure 6 compares the measured peak loads (maximum
instantaneous and maximum 15 minute interval average) to
the design loads for various laboratory spaces in a building
at the University of California, Davis. Although the sizing
ratio (design/measured) is driven by context specific fac-
tors such as reliability and flexibility, it is recommended
that sizing ratios greater than two be carefully evaluated
and justified.
April 2008
ASHRAE Journal
47
Conclusion
Laboratories are more likely to meet energy-efficiency goals
if quantitative metrics and targets are explicitly identified and
tracked during the course of design, delivery, and operation.
This article described key metrics and benchmarks at the
whole building level, as well as at the system level.
Although Standard 90.1-2004 can effectively be used as a
basis for evaluating whole building performance, it is recom-
mended that it be used in conjunction with all the most recent
addenda as well, as the Labs21 modeling guidelines to address
some lab-specific issues such as equipment load diversity and
fan power limitations.
It is strongly recommended that whole building targets be
evaluated against empirical benchmarks that are based on the
measured energy use of peer facilities.
Key ventilation system metrics include: minimum air change
rate (ACH, cfm/ft2 [L/s·m2]), hood density (hoods/net square
feet [hoods/m2]), hood airflow efficiency, system airflow ef-
ficiency (W/cfm [W/L·s–1]).
Heating and cooling system efficiency metrics for labora-
tories are not significantly different from those used for other
commercial buildings, although there are some special consid-
erations for laboratories.
Design assumptions for plug loads
should be benchmarked against measured
values in comparable laboratories.
Metrics and benchmarks are key
performance indicators for the quality
of design and operation. To ensure that
they are effectively used, owners and
designers should obtain the buy-in of all
the key stakeholders, incorporate them
into programming documents, and track
them consistently during over the course
of the design process.
Acknowledgments
This article was based on a best prac-
tice guide developed for the Laboratories
for the 21st Century (Labs21) Program,
a federal program sponsored by the U.S.
Department of Energy and U.S. Environ-
mental Protection Agency.

New Light in Crawlspaces
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
C
rawlspaces stink, they rot, and are just plain icky.
Photo 1 shows the modern crawlspace, which is a forest
of water droplets on the underside of fiberglass batt in-
sulation. The exposed wood floor joists are rotting. This vented
crawlspace has a continuous plastic sheet ground cover and
excellent drainage (Photo 2). Everything in this crawlspace was
done “correctly.” It has code-specified ventilation, a continuous
impermeable ground cover, excellent drainage, but still we have
a mess. What caused this mess? The floor insulation.
Think of the good old days, before WWII, crawlspaces were
uninsulated. They were ventilated, they didn’t have ground
covers, and they didn’t have problems. Why? The floor framing
was usually always warmer than the ground (Figure 1), even in
air-conditioned buildings. This was a big deal as the temperature
of the floor framing was above the dew-point temperature of the
exterior air used for ventilation. The old floor finishes tended
breathe. They were relatively vapor open. No one had heard of
vinyl flooring yet.
The ground in crawlspaces is cold,\* much colder than the
outside air during the summer.
In an irony not appreciated except by building science geeks,
ventilation air in the summer in most parts of North America
brings moisture into crawlspaces and deposits this moisture on
surfaces below the ventilation air dew point. In the good old
days, this was the ground or the ground cover, which is at the
same temperature as the ground. It was not the floor framing.
And, who cared if the ground or the ground cover was wet
as long as the wood framing was not. Hardly any ventilation
air change occurs in crawlspaces. The typical ventilation air
change rate in a crawlspace is approximately 1 air change per
hour (ach).† In determining crawlspace surface temperatures
we can pretty much ignore the ventilation air change.‡ We
can’t ignore the ventilation air in the moisture balance but we
can in the energy balance. Crawlspace surface temperatures
are dominated by radiation and conduction, not by convection
(Figure 2). And, as pointed out, old crawlspace floor framing
was not only warmer than the ground but also warmer than the
ventilation air dew point.
That all changes when we install insulation in crawlspace
floor framing. The most common insulation installed in this
location are fiberglass batts. When fiberglass batts are installed
between floor joists the exposed bottom edges of the floor joists
become much colder (Figure 3). The surface temperature of the
underside of the fiberglass batt insulation is also much colder
than the floor sheathing, within one or two degrees of the ground
temperature. The energy picture within the crawlspace is radia-
tion dominated. The floor assembly surfaces are, in essence,
radiation coupled to the ground. The exposed surface of the
fiberglass batt insulation is below the dew-point temperature of
the air in the crawlspace as is the exposed portion of the wood
floor joists. Condensation forms on both the surface of the
insulation and the surface of the exposed wood (Photo 1).
Now, let’s look at the wood floor joist moisture content. Wood
is hygroscopic. It “sees” relative humidity, not vapor pressure.
We need a psychrometric chart and a sorption curve for wood
(see Page 70). The temperature of the wood floor joist is 75°F
\*A reasonable rule-of-thumb to estimate crawlspace ground surface temperatures is to use the average annual ambient air temperature for that location.
†This comes from using radon gas as a “tracer gas”—thank you EPA. Your radon studies provided useful information, at least in this regard.
‡This is another one of those engineering approximations that drives energy weenies crazy.
Photo 1 (left): Icky crawlspace. Note the condensation on the underside of the fiberglass batt insulation and the rot at the exposed portions
of the crawlspace floor joists. Photo 2 (right): Ventilation opening. Crawlspace has plenty of cross ventilation and good drainage.

(24°C) at the top (i.e., interior temperature). We need to figure
out the crawlspace conditions. The air in the crawlspace comes
from the outside, who knew? Let’s pretend this particular
crawlspace is in Washington, D.C. The average dew point of
the exterior air during the summer in Washington, D.C., is 65°F
(18°C). Let’s bring this air into the crawlspace. Therefore, the
dew point of the air in the crawlspace is 65°F (18°C). Remem-
ber that the top of the floor joist is 75°F (24°C). The floor joist
sees the dew point of the air in the crawlspace (we can ignore
the vapor permeance characteristics of the fiberglass batt in-
sulation since it is so vapor open. Simply pretend we have air,
rather than insulation here. However, not just any air, air with
a huge temperature drop—“insulating air”), but because the
floor joist is 75°F (24°C) at this location, the relative humidity
is 70%, yielding a wood equilibrium moisture content of 13%.
The floor joist is “dry” at the top and “wet” at the bottom. Why
wet at the bottom? The surface of the wood is cold, below the
dew point of the air in the crawlspace. Therefore, condensation
Figure 1: Old crawlspaces. Old uninsulated and ventilated crawl-
spaces had warm floor assembly surfaces due to heat flow downward
from occupied space above.
Figure 2: Old crawlspace temperatures. Surface temperatures are
dominated by radiation and conduction, not by convection. Old
crawlspace floor framing was not only warmer than the ground but
also warmer than the ventilation air dew point.
forms on the wood. At fiber saturation, the moisture content of
wood is 28% (Figure 3).
The wood floor joist moisture content increases downward
as the wood becomes progressively colder (Figure 4). Another
way of saying this is the warmer the wood, the drier the wood.
If we were to wrap the floor joists completely with insulation,
we would warm the wood, lowering the relative humidity the
wood “sees,” which lowers the moisture content. This is a neat
strategy that I will refer to later. How low do we need to lower
the moisture content of the wood? Below 19% to never see rot
or below 16% to never see mold. We could do this a couple of
ways. One way is to use spray foam insulation (Figure 5).
Before we go there, we need to check out something else.
Although the wood moisture content increases as we go
Figure 4: Moisture dynamics. The wood moisture content increases
as we move down the floor joist. At the same time, the vapor drive
is upward. The entire shaded area “sees” the same vapor pressure
(dew point) due to the vapor openness of the fiberglass insulation.
The entire vapor resistance is at the floor sheathing and flooring.
If only the floor could breathe as it did in the old days when we had
wood floors and not vinyl.
Figure 3: Insulated crawlspace temperatures. The surface tem-
perature of the underside of the fiberglass batt insulation is
colder than the floor sheathing—within one or two degrees of
the ground temperature. The exposed surface of the fiberglass
batt insulation is below the dew-point temperature of the air in
the crawlspace as is the exposed portion of the wood floor joists.
Condensation forms on the surface of the insulation and the
surface of the exposed wood.

downward, the vapor drive is upward. Huh? This wood stuff is
weird. I just explained wood moisture content. It’s time to look
at vapor diffusion.
The interior is dry (dew point of 55°F [13°C]), and the
crawlspace is wet (dew point of 65°F [18°C]). The moisture
flow by vapor diffusion is from the crawlspace up through
the floor into the building, at least during summer. The vapor
drive is upward, even though the wood in the floor joist be-
comes wetter as we go downward. The inward drive is “felt”
completely at the floor sheathing and floor finish. We can
ignore the vapor open fiberglass batt cavity insulation. No
vapor resistance here. We can’t ignore the vapor resistance
of the floor sheathing and the floor finish.
If we were do something foolish like install vinyl flooring
(0.1 to 0.5 perms) over OSB floor sheathing (1.0 perms) in
an assembly such as that described in Figure 4, we would get
a result that looks like Photo 3. Notice the pink spots on the
vinyl flooring. It reminds you of the pink spots on vinyl wall-
covering.§ Come to think of it, haven’t we just constructed a
wall with vinyl wall-covering and laid it down? Same principle,
same problem. The floor does not “breathe.” We have a vapor
barrier on the wrong side of the assembly.
We can fix this problem by having a vapor permeable floor
assembly. Plywood sheathing (it is way more permeable than
OSB) and carpet do the trick. Works, but it is kind of limiting. Oh,
yeah, you also can’t put furniture on the floor if the furniture is
impermeable, so you have to hold it up to ventilate under it (same
for cabinetry). You have to ventilate it as well. But, what if I don’t
§The pink color comes from digestive enzymes exuded from mold that react with the plasticizers in the vinyl. The mold comes from the substrate being wet because
of the impermeability of the vinyl. No vinyl no problem.
Psychrometric Chart & Wood Sorption Curve
We know the dew-point temperature of the air in the
crawlspace is 65°F (18°C). We also know the temperature at
the top of the floor joist is 75°F (24°C). We need to get the
relative humidity at the surface of the wood at this location,
so that we can use the wood sorption curve to determine
moisture content. The psychrometric chart’s magic gives
us the relative humidity.
First, we make the simple engineering assumption that we
can ignore the vapor permeance (or vapor resistance) of the
fiberglass batt insulation. That leads to the second assumption
that the vapor pressure in the crawlspace is uniform. Both are
reasonable assumptions within the range of accuracy we are
dealing with (within 10% to 20% and obtained within seconds,
rather than hours using mind numbing numerical simulations
based on questionable boundary conditions that are not much
more accurate than 10% to 20%).
With the constant vapor pressure assumption, we can trace
a horizontal line from the 65°F (18°C) dew-point state-point
on the saturation curve to where it crosses the vertical 75°F
(24°C) dry-bulb temperature line. Presto! We get the relative hu-
midity at the surface of the wood, which is 70% (Figure A).
We take this wonderful piece of information to the wood
sorption curve and get a moisture content of 13% (Figure
B). However, look at the error bar (the shading on the curve).
Neat, eh! Wood is somewhat unpredictable—a characteristic
I identify with—so although I say in the neighboring column
that the wood moisture content is 13%, it could be anywhere
between 10% and 16%. Or, 13% ± 3%. Keep those computer
simulations and give me a psych chart, a sorption curve,
and a beer, and I will beat you to the answer every time. Of
course, the 30 years of being beat up by the old masters also
helps (thank you Dr. Onysko, Prof. Timusk, Mr. Gatley, and
Mr. Handegord).

Photo 3: Vinyl flooring. Pink spots are
due to moisture problems arising from
the impermeability of the vinyl flooring
and ventilated and insulated crawlspace
underneath.
want plywood, carpet and ventilated furniture and cabinetry?
The easy answer is to construct a conditioned crawlspace
(Photo 4). Construct it as a mini-basement. Then, you can have
any floor finish and save energy and money. But, I know you
folks. You are stubborn and insist on the vented crawlspace. Hey,
maybe you need to use one because you are in a flood zone. It
could be you actually have a legitimate reason to construct a
vented crawlspace. So, how should the floor assembly look?
Check out Figures 6 and 7. Both show foil-faced rigid insula-
tion under the floor framing. The wood is warm and, therefore,
dry. The foil facing on the rigid insulation handles the vapor
drive. The foil facing is an exceptional vapor barrier (< 0.1
perm). Beautiful. It gets better. If you expose the shiny side
(face it down into the crawlspace) of the foil facing, it almost
eliminates radiative coupling and means that the surface of the
insulation approaches the temperature of the ventilation air,
reducing condensation. Why don’t we see lots of this? Well, I
forgot to mention that the rigid insulation needs to be airtight.
That requires tiny people with good workmanship to seal the
seams with foil tape that must stick in miserable environmental
Photo 4: Conditioned crawlspace. This is the
way all crawlspaces should look—dry, warm,
part of the house, not part of the outside or
part of the ground. Insulated on the perimeter,
not in the floor. Beautiful.
conditions forever. Having said that, a poor job using the ap-
proaches described in Figures 6 and 7 is better than what we
typically get with Photo 1 and Figure 3.
All of this leads us back to Figure 5 and spray foam. Lots of
folks are looking at this option due to the lack of tiny people with
good workmanship.II The spray foam clearly handles warming
the wood. Any foam will work for the wood warming: low den-
sity, high density, whatever. Remember that just warming the
wood lowers its equilibrium moisture content. Where it becomes
difficult is with the vapor drive across the floor sheathing and
the floor finish. Unless you stick to carpet, and ventilated fur-
niture and ventilated cabinetry you must use high-density foam
(at least 2 lb/ft3 [32 kg/m3]) due to its lower perm value. The
foam needs to be at least 3 in. (76 mm) thick or thicker (gives
you less than 1 perm at this thickness). Figure 8 shows a few
configurations that pretty much work everywhere. One limita-
tion, Figure 8d, with vinyl flooring, probably does not work in
mixed humid and hot humid climates. I included it because it
IIThey all have gone on to fame and fortune in reality TV cable shows.
Figure 5: Warming the wood. Wrapping the floor
framing in foam insulation lowers the equilib-
rium moisture content of the wood. Warm wood
is dry wood. Warm wood is happy wood. One not
so minor issue is that changing the temperature
only gets you so far. The vapor drive upwards
still needs to be addressed. The section line is the
location of the cross-sections in Figure 8, which
addresses the not so minor vapor drive issue.
Spray Foam Insulation
Figure 6 (left): Vapor barrier. Installing impermeable rigid insulation keeps the wood framing warm and provides a low perm layer that
addresses the upward vapor drive. How impermeable? It’s less than 0.1 perms. Foil-faced rigid insulations are the ticket here. This approach
allows any type of flooring to be used above. Even better is exposing the shiny side of the foil facing (face it down into the crawlspace)
which almost eliminates radiative coupling and means that the surface of the insulation approaches the temperature of the ventilation air,
reducing condensation. Figure 7 (right): Cavity insulation with vapor barrier. Adding impermeable foil-faced insulating sheathing over
fiberglass cavity insulation is a hybrid approach that uses the best qualities of both materials. Note that the optimum location for the air-
space is above the cavity insulation. Are you folks at the EPA ENERGY STAR® program paying attention? This makes for warmer floors.
This is the same detail that should be used under bedrooms over garages.

does seem to work in dry places and in cold places. I think you
should just say no to vinyl, which makes things much easier.
If you really like vinyl, go with Figures 6 or 7 or, even better,

At the end of the day, remember that the best crawlspace
of all is filled with concrete and called a slab or dug out and
called a basement.

Energy Harvesting
For Wireless Sensors
A
variety of wireless-enabled sensor products, most nota-
bly wireless temperature sensors, have come to market
  recently, both within and outside of the HVAC industry.
They offer the potential to reduce the installed cost of communi-
cating sensed data by replacing wired infrastructure with digital
radio-based communications (e.g., using the ZigBee protocol).
Wireless systems tend to be a more attractive solution in
retrofit situations, where running additional wires often costs
much more than in new installations and also disrupts building
occupants.1 Examples of building applications where wireless
systems can have significant value include:
• Additional temperature sensors to enable building diag-
nostics (e.g., ongoing commissioning);2
• Relocating temperature sensors to improve occupant com-
fort in reconfigured spaces; and
• Occupancy sensors for lighting (and potentially HVAC)
control.
Almost all fully wireless sensor products use batteries to
power the sensor and the on-board radio. Currently, the lifetime
of wireless sensors is limited by the amount of energy that
the batteries can store within the device packaging volume.
Consequently, the batteries require intermittent replacement
to keep running, necessitating ongoing battery monitoring and
maintenance. This decreases the value of wireless sensing solu-
tions and limits their use, particularly for sensors that consume
more energy or applications using higher data rates.\*
Energy harvesting—also known as power scavenging—
devices have the potential to address the limited lifetime of
batteries. In contrast to batteries, they harvest energy from one
or more ambient energy sources (discussed in the following
section) to power the wireless sensor. Energy harvesting sensors
consist of several components:
• Energy harvesting device—Converts ambient energy into
electric energy.
• Energy storage—Stores power generated by the harvesting
device and powers the system; typically a battery,† it could also
be a capacitor.
• Power electronics—Conditions the harvested electric en-
ergy to maximize the amount of electric energy stored; controls
power flow to the sensor and the wireless radio.
• Sensor.
• Wireless radio—Communicates sensed data to another
device.
Types of Energy Harvesting Devices
Energy harvesting devices can harvest different kinds of
energy, including radio frequency, solar, thermal, and vibration.
In addition, a single device can harvest energy from multiple
ambient energy sources.3,4
Radio frequency (RF) devices harvest electromagnetic radia-
tion emitted by radio devices, such as wireless radio networks.
As the most commonly used frequencies are well known, the
devices have an antenna and circuitry tuned to maximize energy
harvesting at these frequencies. Unfortunately, typical electric
field strengths are weak (unless located close to sources), which
severely limits the quantity of energy that can be harvested, e.g.,
to approximately two orders of magnitude less than indoor solar
and thermoelectric devices. Active systems attempt to overcome
this limit by broadcasting RF energy, as is done currently for
RFID tags used in commerce.5
Solar energy harvesting devices use photovoltaic (PV) cells to
convert incident light into electricity. As such, they leverage the
extensive investments made and progress achieved in increasing
the efficiency and reducing the cost of PV for building- and
utility-scale power. Solar devices can produce energy from both
outdoor and indoor light sources, although outdoor insolation
levels yield approximately two to three orders of magnitude
more electricity per unit area than indoor electric light sourc-
es.5,6 Relative to other sources, solar devices can achieve high
energy densities when used in direct sun, but will not function
in areas without light (e.g., highly shaded areas, ducts). Roughly,
a 100 mm2 (0.16 in.2) photovoltaic cell under office lighting
yields approximately 100 µW of power.5,6 Applications to date
include contact and motion sensors for building applications,7
as well as calculators, PDAs, and wristwatches.5
Thermoelectric [TE] devices harvest energy developed from
temperature differences via the Seebeck effect, i.e., a tempera-
ture difference at the junction of two metals or semiconductors
causes current to flow across the junction. The power density
of TE increases with the temperature difference,3,5 increasing
By Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
\* Radio transmissions are energy intensive, so systems typically attempt to minimize radio “on”
time by limiting data transfer and turning on radios for the minimum time required.
† These batteries are much smaller than those used as the sole energy source in conventional
wireless sensors.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 2008.
©Copyright 2008 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational
purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed
electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
86
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
M a y 2 0 0 8
their attractiveness in applications such as sun/shade installa-
tions, compressors, and hot/cold/air/water/refrigerant flows. As
most in-building HVAC applications for wireless sensors tend
to have smaller temperature differences, this limits the power
density of TE. They have been used to power wristwatches5
and at least one small-scale wireless sensor system is available
for building applications.8 Multiple providers of TE energy
harvesting devices exist.
Vibration harvesters extract energy from ambient vibrations. As
power usually scales with the cube of vibration frequency and the
square of vibration amplitude, these devices have the most value
in applications with high-frequency, high-amplitude vibrations.5
Thus, potentially promising HVAC&R vibration sources could
include compressors, motors, pumps, blowers, and, perhaps to a
lesser extent, fans and ducts. All vibration-harvesting systems
contain mechanical elements that vibrate, typically a spring-mass
assembly with its natural frequency close to those of the vibration
source to maximize energetic coupling between the vibration
source and the harvesting system. Several different ways exist
to translate the vibrating elements into electric energy, including
piezoelectric, capacitive, and inductive systems.6
Piezoelectric materials generate current flow in response to
mechanical strain, such as occurs in a vibrating cantilevered
beam with a mass at the end. Thus, vibrations in the piezoelectric
material repeatedly load and unload the material to produce an
alternating voltage. In capacitive systems, the vibration causes
the gap or overlapping area between two plates to change. This
changes the capacitance and, hence, the voltage difference across
the capacitor.6
Presently, researchers are developing capacitive and piezo-
electric microelectromechanical systems (MEMS),3,5,6 and
piezoelectric-based wireless sensor systems have been produced
at scales relevant to building systems.5,7
Inductive systems harness the vibrations to generate relative
motion between a permanent magnet and a wire coil. This mo-
tion induces a current flow in the coil that powers the system.
Although they appear to not have been deployed yet in building
systems, they have been deployed in other applications, including
self-winding watches, in-shoe power generation, shake-driven
flashlights,5 and industrial equipment.9
To date, a limited number of energy harvesting products exist for
building applications. Light- or vibration-powered products exist,
primarily for temperature sensing, but also for window open/closed
status, window blind actuators, occupancy sensing, light level sens-
ing, and presence sensing.7 Research efforts target a wider set of
applications, including monitoring bridges,5 automobile tires,3,5
bore-hole equipment,3 airplane compres-
sors and turbines,3,5 underwater flows,5
military systems,4,10 industrial machinery,9
medical devices,4 self-powered sporting
goods and in-shoe power.5
Market Factors
Energy harvesting is truly an emerging
technology for HVAC&R applications,
with negligible market penetration to
date. As such, the costs tend to be quite
high. Moreover, the low levels of energy
harvested within the packaging constraints
of HVAC&R sensors limit the application
of energy harvesting power systems to
sensors that consume low levels of energy
and require infrequent data transfer, such
as space temperature measurements.
In spite of these challenges, favorable
trends in key enabling technologies will
help to address the limitations of current
energy harvesting systems. Work to im-
prove power management, combined with
the development of more efficient energy
harvesting circuitry, will decrease the en-
ergy required to power wireless systems.3,5
This, in turn, allows designers to decrease
system size and, hence, cost. Alternatively,
it permits designers to meet the needs of
applications that require higher power draw,
e.g., for either the sensor or more frequent
radio broadcasting. Furthermore, ongoing
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
May 2008
ASHRAE Journal
89
research, development, and commercial-
ization of MEMS promise to decrease the
cost and increase the energy collection ef-
ficiency of energy harvesting devices.
Meanwhile, battery technology, most
notably lithium-ion (Li-ion), continues
to advance. This raises the possibility
that, together, improvements in Li-ion
battery, power management, and energy
harvesting technologies could yield wire-
less sensor systems with sufficiently long
lifetimes (e.g., 15 or 20 years) that they
could be the preferred wireless system
power solution of the future.

About the Authors
T
 he Kern Schools Federal Credit Union Administration Building located in
Bakersfield, Calif., is the first LEED®-certified building in California’s Central
Valley. In addition to breaking new ground as the valley’s first LEED building,
this project brought together two unlikely partners—a financial institution and
a developer. Founded in 1938, financial institution Kern Schools Federal Credit
Union often uses the phrase “Together, we have something special.”
This project is special in that it had a
developer that shared Kern School’s vi-
sion for sustainable growth in the valley
that agreed to pursue a LEED certifica-
tion and a design team that included a
founding member of the San Joaquin
Valley Chapter of the U.S. Green Build-
ing Council. Together, the project team
members set a precedent for future build-
ings in the central valley, which has been
under scrutiny for its vast flat spaces and
minimum-efficiency single-story build-
ings. It has been said that developers
are not likely to support the sustainable
movement in California because they
are too driven by first cost. This project
has become an example of responsible
and efficient building for local owners,
designers and developers.
The Kern Schools buildings are two
steel frame, three-story buildings of ap-
proximately 75,000 gross ft2 (6968 m2)
each. The buildings are mirrored in shape,
similar in design, and have exhibited simi-
lar performance characteristics. Because
of the inability to separate the energy use
and cost associated with the large comput-
er center in the west building, this energy
study comprises only information about
the east building, known as the Adminis-
tration Building. Primarily comprised of
class A office space approximately 2,000
ft2 (186 m2) of the Administration Build-
ing first floor is used for a fitness center,
and another 2,750 ft2 (255 m2) is used for a
small café restaurant and dining area used
by the building occupants, as well as the
general public.
Before developing the mechanical sys-
tems, efforts were focused on making the
Credit Union Goes Green

57
building’s construction as energy efficient
as possible, yet within the constraints of
the budget. Improving the building ef-
ficiency first helped reduce the total ton-
nage required and helped guide the final
development of the mechanical systems.
The building walls are constructed with
6 in. (152 mm) metal stud framing with
R-19 batt insulation. The exterior coating
system also uses 2 in. (51 mm) of foam
insulation with a value of R-4 per inch
(mm). The building uses a cool roof sys-
tem with R-30 insulation. High efficiency,
low-e glazing was used on the east, south,
and west sides of the building to limit solar
heat gain while a glazing system with a
higher level of visible light transmittance
was used on the north side
of the building to maxi-
mize the amount of natural
daylighting and minimize
the indoor cooling loads
generated by the lighting
systems.
The general office space
is air conditioned by two
90 ton (317 kW) and one
75 ton (264 kW) pack-
aged variable air volume
(VAV) air handlers on the
roof. Each floor is served
by a separate unit. The
café and fitness center are
served by a fourth 40 ton
(141 kW) packaged VAV unit. The three
main building units use an evaporative
precooled condensing unit, yielding an
EER of approximately 13 in the dry
Bakersfield climate. The fourth unit
serving the café and fitness center is a
high-efficiency, air-cooled unit with an
EER between 9 and 10.
The minimum ventilation provided by
each air handler ranges from 11% to 28%
of the total supply airflow. In addition,
the building is equipped with an energy
management system (EMS) that is tied to
a building-wide carbon dioxide monitor-
ing system that can be used to modulate
the fresh air supplied to the building. Each
air handler uses integrated dry-bulb tem-
perature economizers to allow free-cooling
when outdoor temperatures are lower than
the return air temperature. The supply air
temperature and volume from each air
handler is modulated to allow a high level
of ventilation and comfort throughout the
building. Each of the air-handling units is
equipped with filters with a minimum ef-
ficiency reporting value (MERV) of 13 and
variable frequency drives with premium
efficiency motors are installed on supply
and return fans. The supply air fans are
modulated to maintain a static pressure
setpoint, which is continually reset by the
EMS to reduce fan power while polling
the demands of each VAV box. When 5%
of the VAV boxes reach 98% of capacity or
more, the static pressure setpoint is reset to
increase the fan speed.
Hot water for building heating is sup-
plied by two high-efficiency modulating
condensing boilers. Heat-
ing hot water is pumped
throughout the building
to reheat coils in each of
the VAV boxes serving
the building. A variable
primary pumping system
with multistage pumps
equipped with premium
efficiency motors is used
to take advantage of the
low flow capabilities of
the condensing boilers
and eliminate the need for
any secondary distribution
pumps. The same boilers
also provide domestic hot
water for the building. Heating hot water
is pumped through a heat exchanger in a
large domestic water storage tank. The
domestic hot water is then circulated
throughout the building and controlled by
a line temperature sensor and scheduled by
the building EMS. Figure 1 is a diagram
of the mechanical system.
The design team encountered many
challenges that led to innovative solu-
tions. One example was meeting a
requirement for air conditioning in the
kitchen of the café.
The café is located on the first floor of
the Administration Building. The Type 1
kitchen hood is provided with makeup air
by an indirect evaporative cooler (IEC) on
the roof equipped with a hot water heating
coil. A portion of the supply air from the
indirect cooler is also routed to a 4 ton
(14 kW) split system, consisting of a DX
Photo 1: An energy analysis
for the credit union showed the
anticipated energy consump-
tion to be 24% below baseline
requirements.

air handler located in the ceiling adjacent
to the kitchen area and a condensing unit
on the roof.
The indirect cooler precools the air
before it enters the air handler. This
protects the refrigeration system from
excessively high temperatures. During
the summer months, the IEC and split
system operate to maintain comfortable
temperatures for the kitchen staff. In the
winter months, the circulating water
pump in the IEC is shut off and the heat-
ing coil is used for heating.
Maintaining a high indoor air quality
and comfortable environment for occu-
pants is a high priority for Kern Schools
as well as the LEED certification. Each
air handler uses MERV-13 filters to
remove much of the outdoor dust and
contaminants present in the air, much of
which is due to nearby agriculture.
Pollutants generated indoors were
mitigated through the use of low VOC
materials during construction. Occu-
pant-borne pollutants are controlled by
a building wide CO2 monitoring system,
Figure 1: Mechanical system schematic.
which allows the amount of fresh outside
air to be modulated based on a setpoint
of 650 ppm above ambient CO2 levels.
As the outside air volume modulates,
building pressure is relieved through
the air handler exhaust fans, which are
modulated by static pressure probes
located throughout the facility.
Finally, each night during selected
months of the year, the air-handling sys-
tem enters a purge mode to rid the building
of any lingering contaminants and provide
cooling of the materials and structure to
use the building’s thermal mass for cool-
ing during hot summer days.
Since the building was first occupied
in September 2005, the tenants have
indicated that the level of comfort far
exceeds that of previous buildings they
have occupied.
This building consumes up to 50% less
energy than a comparable building built
in the last 10 years, despite the use of
higher ventilation rates and the inclusion
of a fitness center and restaurant. During
the design phase, energy use of the build-
ing was modeled using energy analysis
software to compare the anticipated
energy consumption against a baseline
building to demonstrate compliance with
California’s energy code, Title 24.
This analysis showed the anticipated
facility energy consumption to be 24%
below baseline requirements stipulated
by California’s energy code resulting in
an annual energy use reduction of about
210,000 kWh (756 GJ) of electricity and
5,400 therms (570 GJ) of natural gas.
The calculated total energy consumption
was 790,771 kWh (2847 GJ).
The building has been in service and
100% occupied for approximately 22
months and electrical energy-consump-
tion data has been compiled. The actual
total electrical energy consumption from
Jan. 6, 2006 to Jan. 5, 2007 was 830,020
kWh (2988 GJ), which is within 5% of
the total energy consumption predicted
during the design phase. The corre-
sponding energy cost for the same period
was $149,637. The monthly energy con-
sumption can be found in Table 1.
This information has been compared
to the energy use and energy cost for
a similar building, also equipped with
a packaged VAV air-handling system
located nearby on California Avenue in
Bakersfield and managed by the same
property management firm.
The California Avenue Building was
constructed in the 1990s and recently
retrofitted with variable speed motors on

supply fans and pumps along with other energy-saving measures.
Both buildings are measured on the same utility rate, and both
have an occupancy of about 150 people.
The electrical energy use of the Kern Schools Administration
Building was about 50% that of the California Avenue Build-
ing and resulted in an annual energy savings of about $90,000.
This savings, applied to the roughly $200,000 premium paid
by Kern Schools for the energy savings measures used that are
applicable to the electrical energy consumption, produces a
simple straight-line payback of a little more than two years.
Table 2 is a comparison of the energy use trends for each
building.
One key concern of Kern Schools was the ability to maintain
the building systems without the need to hire additional staff
or increase the level of complexity of the systems beyond that
which the staff already maintained. Since many of the build-
ings Kern Schools occupies are branch offices of less than
10,000 ft2 (929 m2) in size, most of the mechanical systems
are comprised of single-zone rooftop packaged air condition-
ers equipped with natural gas-fired heating.
The team decided that the mechanical systems used for the
building could not be any more difficult to maintain than their
other existing systems. This presented a significant challenge,
since the options for a central cooling and heating plant or
other common high-efficiency mechanical system studied did
not meet the maintenance criteria. The evaporative precooled
packaged VAV system provides all of the familiar components,
with an exception made for the addition of VFDs and water
treatment for the evaporative precooled condenser.
In accordance with Kern Schools’ desire to building responsi-
bly, it was requested that the use of chemicals in the mechanical
systems be minimized or eliminated wherever possible. This was
also a concern for the design team in pursuit of LEED certifica-
tion. Significant research and discussion with other owners across
the state indicated that the evaporative precooler section of the
VAV units could be maintained without the use of chemicals by
using an electronic water treatment system instead.
The selected electronic system consists of a device that produces
a pulsed, time-varying, induced electric field inside a PVC pipe
that is fit directly inline with the evaporative condenser recirculat-
ing water system. The electric signal changes the way minerals
in the water precipitate, avoiding hard-lime scale by producing a
nonsticking mineral powder in the bulk water. This powder is read-
ily filterable and mostly removed during normal blowdown, or it
settles loosely in the cooling tower basin for easy annual removal.
Bacteria are encapsulated into the mineral powder and leave the
system through blowdown, filtration, or settling.
Without the need for chemicals such as biocides and corro-
sion and scale inhibitors, approximately 120 gallons (454 L)/
year of industrial strength chlorine bleach, 30 – 40 lbs (14 – 18
kg) of aceto diphosphonic acid and 30 – 40 lbs (14 – 18 kg) of
n-alkylated tolytriazole that would normally be discharged by
a system of this size are eliminated.
Site visits and discussions with maintenance staff after one
year of service confirmed that the system functioned as de-

ASHRAE Journal

signed, and that the inside of the precooler
baffles showed no signs of build-up.
The Kern Schools Federal Credit
Union project was in many ways a
proving ground for new technology
and an example to other Owners and
developers in California’s Central Val-
ley. Since this project became LEED
certified, many other developers in the
Valley have taken notice. Recently,
several buildings have filed applications
to become certified.
A valuable lesson learned on the proj-
ect by the entire team was the benefit of
working toward a common goal. Each
member of the team committed to the
LEED process, the additional upfront
costs associated with energy-savings
measures and the chance for a clean,
healthy indoor building environment.
This was evident from the day of the kick-
off meeting to the first day of occupancy.
The many energy-saving and air-quality
features of the mechanical system were
devised by the design team in close co-
ordination with the developer and Kern
Schools. For this reason, the success of
the project could best be summed up by
the phrase “Together, we have something
special.”
